prompt: Delia Okafor chairs the Lakeside Camera Club and is training a new recording secretary. She asks you to explain, as simply as possible, the ordinary sequence of steps by which a motion is brought before the assembly before it can be considered.
gold: A motion is brought before the assembly in three steps. First, a member obtains the floor and makes the motion by saying "I move that...". Second, another member seconds the motion. Third, the chair states the question, formally placing the motion before the assembly. Only after the chair states the question is the motion pending and open to debate.

ref.quote: The three steps by which a motion is normally brought before the assembly are as follows:

prompt: At the Riverton Historical Society, member Paul Reyes made a motion and it was promptly seconded, and he then began debating it before the chair had said anything. President Grace Lin asks you whether the motion was properly before the assembly at that point.
gold: No. Making and seconding a motion do not place it before the assembly; only the chair does that, by stating the question. Until the chair states the question, the motion is not pending and debate is not yet in order. The chair should state the question first, after which the maker may be recognized to speak first in debate.

ref.quote: Neither the making nor the seconding of a motion places it before the assembly; only the chair can do that, by the third step (stating the question).

prompt: Tomas Vega, president of the Glenwood Garden Club, thinks a second means the seconder agrees with the motion, and he wants to know why the rules require one at all. Explain the actual purpose of a second.
gold: The requirement of a second is essentially guidance for the chair on whether to state the question and place the motion before the assembly. Its purpose is to prevent time being consumed on a motion that only one person wishes to introduce. A second merely means the seconder wants the question to come before the meeting; it does not mean the seconder favors the motion.

ref.quote: The requirement of a second is for the chair’s guidance whether to state the question on the motion, thus placing it before the assembly.

prompt: At the Fairhaven Arts Council, the finance committee has directed its chair to move the adoption of the committee's recommendation. Member Nadia Post insists the motion still needs a second from the floor. President Omar Diallo asks you to settle the point.
gold: A motion made by direction of a board or a duly appointed committee of more than one person requires no second from the floor. The committee's decision to bring the motion already reflects the will of at least two members, so the purpose of a second is met. The chair may proceed to state the question without waiting for a second.

ref.quote: A motion made by direction of a board or duly appointed committee of the assembly requires no second from the floor

prompt: During a meeting of the Cedar Valley Kennel Club, a motion was stated by the chair without a second, debate proceeded, and only after two members had spoken did member Ruth Alvarez rise to a point of order that the motion was never seconded. Chair Ben Stroud asks you how to rule.
gold: The point of order comes too late and should not be sustained. Once debate has begun (or, where there is no debate, once any member has voted), the lack of a second becomes immaterial and it is too late to raise the point. A point of order about a missing second must be made before that stage. If a motion is even adopted without a second, its validity is unaffected.

ref.quote: After debate has begun or, if there is no debate, after any member has voted, the lack of a second has become immaterial and it is too late to make a point of order that the motion has not been seconded.

prompt: New president Yuki Tanaka of the Brookside Chess Federation is unsure what she should actually say after a motion is made and seconded. Explain how the chair states the question on an ordinary motion.
gold: After an in-order motion has been made and seconded, the chair states the question, which places it before the assembly. The basic form is, "It is moved and seconded that... [repeating the motion]." The chair then turns toward the maker to see if he wishes the floor first in debate, or asks, "Are you ready for the question?" This indicates the motion is now open to debate.

ref.quote: When a motion that is in order has been made and seconded, the chair formally places it before the assembly by *stating the question*

prompt: At the Northgate Neighborhood Association, member Carl Boone offers a rambling motion whose wording is unclear and would have to be paraphrased for the minutes. President Ada Kelly asks what she should do before stating the question.
gold: When a motion is offered in wording that is unclear or would require smoothing before it can be recorded, it is the chair's duty to see that it is put into suitable form, preserving the content to the mover's satisfaction, before stating the question. The chair must never admit a motion the secretary would have to paraphrase. The chair may require the motion to be put in writing before stating the question.

ref.quote: it is the duty of the chair to see that the motion is put into suitable form

prompt: President Marcus Reed of the Hilltop Rotary must rule a member's motion out of order and wants to know the proper wording. He asks whether he can simply tell the member, "You are out of order."
gold: No. The chair should say, "The chair rules that the motion is not in order because... [briefly stating the reason]." He must not say, "You are out of order," nor, "Your motion is out of order," because to call a member out of order implies misconduct. The ruling is subject to appeal to the assembly.

ref.quote: He must not say, “You are out of order,” nor, “Your motion is out of order.”

prompt: Member Ines Fuchs of the Oakmont Literary Guild made a motion and, before the chair did anything with it, decided she wanted to change its wording. President Leon Marsh asks whether she may do so and until what point.
gold: Until the chair states the question, the maker has the right to modify her motion as she pleases or to withdraw it entirely. Once the chair states the question, the motion becomes the property of the assembly, and after that she can neither modify nor withdraw it without the assembly's consent. While it is pending, any change must be made by amendment.

ref.quote: the maker has the right to modify his motion as he pleases or to withdraw it entirely.

prompt: At the Summit Debate League, member Hana Reyes wants to speak a second time on a pending motion while another member who has not yet spoken is seeking the floor. Chair Devon Pike asks you to explain the debate limits.
gold: Each member has the right to speak twice on the same question on the same day. However, a member cannot make a second speech on the question so long as any member who has not yet spoken on it desires the floor. So the member who has not spoken should be recognized first. A member who has spoken twice has exhausted the right to debate that question that day.

ref.quote: each member has the right to speak twice on the same question on the same day, but cannot make a second speech on the same question

prompt: The Meadowbrook Homeowners Association has no rule of its own about speech length, and a member wants to speak for twenty minutes. President Sofia Grant asks how long a member may speak.
gold: Without the permission of the assembly, no one may speak longer than the body's rules allow. In a nonlegislative assembly that has no rule of its own on the length of speeches, the limit is ten minutes per speech. To exceed that, the member needs the assembly's permission, which can be granted by unanimous consent or by a two-thirds vote.

ref.quote: in a nonlegislative assembly that has no rule of its own relating to the length of speeches, longer than ten minutes.

prompt: During a heated debate at the Westport Civic Forum, member Ryan Cole begins questioning the motives of another member. Chair Priya Nair asks you what the rules of decorum in debate require.
gold: Debate must be confined to the merits of the pending question. Speakers must address their remarks to the chair, keep a courteous tone, and must never attack or allude to the motives of members. They should refer to officers only by title and avoid mentioning members' names. The chair should hold the speaker to discussing the question, not personalities.

ref.quote: they must never attack or make any allusion to the motives of members.

prompt: President Ellis Chan of the Baytown Merchants League feels strongly about a pending motion and wants to argue for it from the chair. He asks whether that is proper in an ordinary assembly.
gold: Except in committees and small boards, the presiding officer does not enter into discussion of the merits of pending questions. To preserve impartiality, the chair should refrain from debating. If he genuinely wishes to advocate, he may relinquish the chair until the pending business is disposed of, but he should not resume presiding over that question.

ref.quote: the presiding officer does not enter into discussion of the merits of pending questions

prompt: At the Pinecrest Alumni Association, President Gwen Sato is tired of a long debate and wants to simply cut it off and take the vote. She asks you whether she has the authority to close debate on her own.
gold: No. The presiding officer cannot close debate so long as any member who has not exhausted the right to debate still desires the floor. Debate can be closed only by order of the assembly, which requires a two-thirds vote (for example, by ordering the Previous Question). The chair may ask whether members are ready for the question, but cannot force debate to end unilaterally.

ref.quote: The presiding officer cannot close debate so long as any member who has not exhausted his right to debate desires the floor

prompt: President Hugo Bassett of the Clearwater Sailing Club took a voice vote, heard a strong chorus of ayes, and started to declare the motion adopted without asking for the noes. A member objects. He asks you whether he must call for the negative vote.
gold: Yes. The chair must always call for the negative vote, no matter how nearly unanimous the affirmative appears. The only common relaxation is for noncontroversial complimentary or courtesy motions, and even then the chair must call for the negative if any member objects. The chair does not call for abstentions, since to abstain is simply not to vote.

ref.quote: The chair must always call for the negative vote, no matter how nearly unanimous the affirmative vote may appear

prompt: At the Ashford Foundation, member Nora Webb voted aye and then wants to switch to no. President Kwame Osei asks you until what point she may change her vote.
gold: A member has the right to change her vote up to the time the chair announces the result. After the result has been announced, she can change it only by unanimous permission of the assembly. So if the result has not yet been announced, she may simply change it; if it has, she must obtain unanimous consent.

ref.quote: a member has the right to change his vote up to the time the result is announced. After that, he can make the change only by unanimous permission of the assembly.

prompt: President Ivy Delgado of the Sandstone Book Circle took a close voice vote and is reasonably convinced of the outcome but worries some members may question it. She asks you how she can announce the result while giving members a chance to object.
gold: When the chair is reasonably convinced but a close result may be questioned, she may first say, "The ayes [or the noes] seem to have it." She then pauses; any member who doubts the result is thereby invited to demand a division (a rising vote). If no one demands verification, she completes the announcement, "The ayes have it...". If she is in actual doubt, she should instead retake the vote by rising.

ref.quote: any member who doubts the result is thus invited to demand verification of the vote by a *division*, as explained below.

prompt: Right after a voice vote at the Elm Street Cooperative, member Frank Odum calls out "Division!" President Tara Bloom asks you what a division is and whether it needs a second.
gold: A demand for a division requires the chair to retake the vote as a rising vote. Any member has this right, without a second, from the moment the negative votes are cast until just after the result is announced, so long as it is not used as a dilatory tactic when the result is clear. The member simply calls out "Division!" and the chair must immediately take a rising vote.

ref.quote: any member (without a second) has the right to require that a voice vote (or even a vote by show of hands) be retaken as a rising vote

prompt: On a counted vote requiring only a majority at the Harborview Association, the members are tied without the presiding officer's vote. President Alan Frost, who is a member, asks you what effect his vote or abstention would have.
gold: When there is a tie without the chair's vote, he may vote in the affirmative, and that vote adopts the motion; if he abstains, the motion is lost because a majority in the affirmative is required and a tie rejects it. The chair, to protect his impartiality, normally votes only when his vote would affect the result. So here he may cast the deciding aye or simply let the tie defeat the motion.

ref.quote: When there is a tie without the chair’s vote, the chair can vote in the affirmative, and such a vote adopts the motion; but if the chair abstains from voting, the motion is lost.

prompt: President Lucia Marsh of the Fernbrook Guild wants to speed up a routine, uncontroversial item without going through a formal vote. She asks you to explain unanimous consent and when it is appropriate.
gold: Unanimous consent is a time-saving method appropriate for routine matters or questions of little importance where there seems to be no opposition. It can be used to adopt a motion without formally stating the question and taking a vote, or even to take action with no motion at all. The chair says, "If there is no objection..." and, hearing none, declares the action taken. If any member objects, the chair must proceed with a regular vote.

ref.quote: the method of unanimous consent can be used either to adopt a motion without the steps of stating the question and putting the motion to a formal vote, or it can be used to take action without even the formality of a motion.

prompt: At the Willowdale Society, President Sam Okonkwo proposed handling a matter by unanimous consent, but member Beth Cardoza called out "I object." He asks you what he must do now.
gold: Once a member objects, the chair cannot use unanimous consent and must proceed formally. He must state the question on the motion, allow any desired debate (unless the motion is undebatable), and put the question in the regular manner. If no motion was actually made, he must first call for one or at least put the question assuming such a motion. An objection made with reasonable promptness must be honored even if he already announced "no objection."

ref.quote: If any member objects, the chair must state the question on the motion, allow any desired debate

prompt: During a unanimous-consent request at the Grafton Club, member Dana Willow is unsure of the effect of the proposed action but does not want to block it outright. President Cyrus Vale asks you what she can do.
gold: If a member is uncertain of the effect of an action proposed for unanimous consent, she can call out, "I reserve the right to object," or, "Reserving the right to object,...". After a brief consultation to clarify matters, she can then either object or withdraw her reservation. Members should not object merely for dilatory purposes, but no one should hesitate to object if they feel a formal vote is desirable.

ref.quote: he can call out, “I reserve the right to object,”

prompt: New member orientation leader Grace Holt at the Verdant Society is asked to define a main motion and to state the one basic condition on when it may be made. Explain it plainly.
gold: A main motion is a motion whose introduction brings business before the assembly; it is the basic device by which a substantive matter is presented for action. Its defining condition is that it can be made only while no other motion is pending. If something else is already pending, a main motion is out of order until that business is disposed of.

ref.quote: a *main motion* is a motion whose introduction brings business before the assembly; such a motion can be made only while no other motion is pending.

prompt: President Milo Frost of the Brightwater Association believes every main motion needs a two-thirds vote. He asks you what vote a main motion ordinarily requires and the main exceptions.
gold: A main motion ordinarily requires only a majority vote. Exceptions arise when the bylaws or special rules prescribe a higher requirement or previous notice; when adoption would suspend a rule of order or a parliamentary right of members (two-thirds); or when it would change something already adopted, in which case the vote to Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted applies. Absent such a case, a simple majority adopts the motion.

ref.quote: Requires a majority vote, except:

prompt: A member of the Coastline League wants to move that the society "reaffirm" a resolution it adopted last year. President Rhea Colton asks you whether such a motion is proper.
gold: No, a motion to reaffirm a position previously adopted is not in order. The original motion is still in effect, so reaffirming it serves no useful purpose. It also creates problems, because attempts to amend a reaffirmation would conflict with the rules for Amend Something Previously Adopted, and if the reaffirmation failed it would create an ambiguous situation. The prior resolution simply remains in force.

ref.quote: Motions to “reaffirm” a position previously taken by adopting a motion or resolution are not in order.

prompt: At the Redfield Association, a member proposes to move "that no response be made" to a request for a donation. President Oscar Lund asks you whether such a motion is appropriate.
gold: Ordinarily not. A motion whose only effect is to propose that the assembly refrain from doing something should not be offered if the same result can be achieved by offering no motion at all. Simply making no motion accomplishes taking no action. Such a negative motion is proper only if some purpose is served, for example to bind a subordinate body from acting later, or to place on record the reasons why no action should be taken.

ref.quote: A motion whose only effect is to propose that the assembly *refrain* from doing something should not be offered if the same result can be accomplished by offering no motion at all.

prompt: After the Lakemont Society voted down a resolution declaring support for a bond issue, a member argues the society is now officially on record as opposing it. President Vera Quin asks you whether that is correct.
gold: No. Voting down a resolution expressing a particular opinion is not the same as adopting one expressing the opposite opinion; when a motion is defeated, neither opinion has been expressed. A member may even agree with the resolution's views yet vote against it because the organization should not speak out at all. So the society has taken no position, not the opposite position.

ref.quote: voting down a motion or resolution that would express a particular opinion is not the same as adopting a motion expressing the opposite opinion, since—if the motion is voted down—neither opinion has been expressed.

prompt: President Iris Vaughn of the Northfield Council thinks every resolution must open with "Whereas" clauses. She asks you when a preamble should be used.
gold: Neither rule nor custom requires a resolution to have a preamble, and one should not be used merely for the sake of form. Including reasons within a motion can encumber it and cost votes from members who dislike the stated reasons. A preamble should be limited to cases where it supplies little-known information without which the resolution's point or merits would be poorly understood, or where making the reasons a matter of record is unusually important.

ref.quote: the use of a preamble should be limited to cases where it provides little-known information without which the point or the merits of a resolution are likely to be poorly understood

prompt: At the Stonebridge Society, a member offered a single main motion adopting a series of resolutions on completely unrelated subjects. Another member wants Resolution No. 2 considered by itself. President Hal Brenner asks you how to handle it.
gold: Where a single main motion offers a series of independent resolutions on completely different subjects, any resolution in the series must be taken up and voted on separately at the demand of a single member. That demand can be made even when another has the floor, at any time until the series has been voted on, by saying, "I call for a separate vote on Resolution No. 2." (If the resolutions all relate to one subject, separating them instead requires the motion for Division of a Question, adopted by majority vote.)

ref.quote: any resolution in the series must be taken up and voted on separately at the demand of a single member.

prompt: Earlier in the same meeting of the Maple Ridge Club, a motion was rejected. Now a member wants to introduce essentially the same proposal again. President Joan Petty asks you whether that is in order.
gold: No. A main motion presenting substantially the same question as one finally disposed of earlier in the same session (by being rejected, postponed indefinitely, or subjected to a sustained objection to consideration) is out of order. However, the question can be renewed at any later session as if new, or a motion to Reconsider the disposing vote may be made for a limited time during the same session. Reconsideration is the route to revisit it within this meeting.

ref.quote: No main motion is in order that presents substantially the same question as a motion that was finally disposed of earlier in the same session by being rejected, postponed indefinitely

prompt: President Ravi Menon of the Oakhurst Association keeps hearing that certain motions need "previous notice" but is not sure what that means or how it is given. Explain it.
gold: A requirement of previous notice means that an announcement that the motion will be introduced, indicating its content, must be given ahead of time. It must be included in the call of the meeting at which the motion will be taken up, or, if no more than a quarterly interval will have passed, it may be announced at the preceding meeting. This gives members fair warning that the question is coming up.

ref.quote: A requirement of previous notice means that announcement that the motion will be introduced

prompt: The board of the Kingsley Foundation took action at a meeting where no quorum was present, and President Elena Ward wants to know how the organization can make that action valid. Explain the appropriate motion.
gold: The organization should use the motion to ratify, an incidental main motion that confirms or makes valid an action already taken that could not become valid until the assembly approved it. It applies to situations such as action taken without a quorum or beyond someone's authority. Because it is a main motion, it is debatable and opens the whole question to debate. The assembly can ratify only what it could have authorized in advance.

ref.quote: The motion to *ratify* (also called *approve* or *confirm*) is an incidental main motion that is used to confirm or make valid an action already taken that cannot become valid until approved by the assembly.

prompt: At the Ferndale Historical Society, a member has moved to postpone indefinitely a controversial resolution endorsing a political candidate. President Alicia Reyes asks you to explain to the assembly what adopting this motion would actually accomplish, since some members think it merely delays the matter. Advise her.
gold: Postpone Indefinitely does not delay anything; if adopted it kills the main motion for the rest of the session and prevents a direct vote on it. It is useful for disposing of a badly chosen main motion that the assembly would rather not adopt or expressly reject. Members should understand that a vote to postpone indefinitely is in effect an indirect rejection of the resolution.

ref.quote: Its adoption kills the main motion (for the duration of the session) and avoids a direct vote on the question.

prompt: During a meeting of the Lakeside Rowing Club, a motion to Postpone Indefinitely is pending on a main motion. Chair Tom Vance wants to know how far debate on the postponement may range, and whether members may argue the merits of the underlying main motion. Explain.
gold: Postpone Indefinitely is debatable, and it is unique among the subsidiary motions in that debate on it may go fully into the merits of the main question itself. This is why opponents of a measure sometimes use it, since it lets them argue against the main motion without risking its adoption. So the chair should allow debate to reach the merits of the underlying resolution.

ref.quote: Is debatable; and, unlike the case of any other subsidiary motion

prompt: Delegate Maria Okafor tells the credentials committee she wants to gauge how much support a proposal has before the final vote, and asks whether Postpone Indefinitely can serve that purpose. Explain the strategic use of this motion.
gold: Postpone Indefinitely is sometimes employed by strategists to test their strength on a motion they oppose. Because debate on it may reach the merits of the main question, members who have used up their debate time on the main motion can speak again. If the indefinite postponement carries, the main motion is suppressed for the session; if it fails, its opponents have learned their strength and still get a vote on the main motion itself.

ref.quote: to test their strength on a motion they oppose

prompt: At the Oakmont Neighborhood Association, a motion to purchase a new desk for the secretary is pending, and a member moves to amend by adding funds for the president's travel to a convention. Chair David Lin asks whether that amendment is in order. Advise him on the governing principle.
gold: An amendment must always be germane, meaning it must be closely related to or have bearing on the subject of the motion to be amended, so that no new subject can be smuggled in under the guise of an amendment. Adding the president's travel expenses introduces an independent question unrelated to buying a desk, so it is not germane and the chair should rule it out of order.

ref.quote: closely related to or having bearing on the subject of the motion to be amended

prompt: The Westbrook Foundation is considering a bylaw amendment that itself requires a two-thirds vote to adopt. A member offers a primary amendment to change one figure in it. Secretary Joan Petrie asks what vote the amendment needs. Explain.
gold: The subsidiary motion to Amend requires only a majority vote, regardless of the vote required to adopt the underlying question. This holds even where adopting the amendment would change the vote needed for the main question. So the amendment to the figure is decided by a majority, even though the bylaw amendment itself will still require two-thirds.

ref.quote: Requires a majority vote, regardless of the vote required to adopt the question to be amended.

prompt: At the Riverdale Choral Guild, a primary amendment and a secondary amendment are both pending, and a member now wishes to amend the secondary amendment. Chair Ben Ortiz asks you whether that is allowed and what the member's options are. Advise him.
gold: Only two degrees of amendment are permitted; an amendment of the third degree is not permitted, so a secondary amendment cannot be amended. The member cannot move to amend the pending secondary amendment. Instead, while the secondary amendment is pending, the member may state that if it is voted down he will offer a different secondary amendment in its place.

ref.quote: An amendment of the third degree is not permitted.

prompt: At the Central Valley Growers Cooperative, a member wants to replace the entire text of a pending resolution with completely new wording. Chair Ellen Marsh asks what form of amendment this is and whether it is a distinct motion. Explain.
gold: This is a motion to substitute, which is one of the forms of the subsidiary motion to Amend: it strikes out a paragraph, or the entire text of a resolution or main motion, and inserts another in its place. It is not a separate motion but a form of Amend, so it needs a second, is debatable when the main motion is, and requires only a majority vote. The term substitute applies only to a complete paragraph or more, not to a change of mere words.

ref.quote: to strike out a paragraph, or the entire text of a resolution or main motion, and insert another in its place

prompt: At the Harborview Yacht Club, a member has moved to strike out certain words and insert others in one motion, and another member now wants the striking-out voted on separately from the inserting. Chair Rita Cho asks whether the motion can be split. Advise her.
gold: A motion to strike out and insert is an indivisible combination of the two acts, and its two parts cannot be separated, either by secondary amendment or by a Division of a Question. The value of this form is precisely that some members will vote to strike out only if assured of the replacement wording. So the chair should decline to divide it and put the combined motion as one question.

ref.quote: The two parts of this motion cannot be separated, either by secondary amendment

prompt: The Summit City Council has pending a motion that its delegates be instructed to vote in favor of a dues increase. A member moves to insert the word 'not' before 'be,' so it reads 'not be instructed.' Chair Marcus Webb asks whether this amendment is proper. Advise him.
gold: An amendment is improper if it merely makes adoption of the amended question equivalent to a rejection of the original motion. Inserting 'not' before 'be' would make an affirmative vote on not giving the instruction identical to a negative vote on giving it, so it is out of order. However, it would be in order to insert 'not' after 'to' so the motion reads 'instructed not to vote in favor,' since that gives a different, positive instruction.

ref.quote: One that merely makes the adoption of the amended question equivalent to a rejection of the original motion.

prompt: At the Greenfield Public Library board, a member has moved to refer a pending policy question to a committee, and debate begins to drift into the merits of the policy itself. Chair Priya Nadella asks you to confirm the standard characteristics of the motion to Commit and how she should confine debate. Explain.
gold: The motion to Commit requires a second, is debatable, is amendable, and needs a majority vote. However, its debate can extend only to the desirability of committing the main question and to the appropriate details of the referral, not to the merits of the main question. So the chair should keep discussion focused on whether and how to refer, ruling out argument on the underlying policy.

ref.quote: The debate can extend only to the desirability of committing the main question

prompt: At the Pinehurst Garden Society, the assembly has just adopted a bare motion 'to refer the question to a committee' with no other details supplied. Chair Alan Frost asks how to complete the incomplete motion when members disagree about which committee and how many members. Advise him.
gold: When a referral lacks details, the chair completes it by first asking to what committee the question shall be referred, treating competing proposals as in filling blanks. If different committee types are proposed, they are voted on in a fixed order: committee of the whole, quasi committee of the whole, informal consideration, standing committees in the order proposed, then special committees (largest membership first) until one gets a majority. For a special committee the chair then settles size and method of selection the same way, and instructions can be added by majority vote.

ref.quote: “To what committee shall the question be referred?”

prompt: The Brookdale Alumni Association voted an hour ago to refer a proposal to a special committee, and now a member wants to reconsider that vote. Chair Nina Alvarez asks whether reconsideration is still available. Advise her.
gold: An affirmative vote on the motion to Commit can be reconsidered only if the committee has not yet begun consideration of the question. Once the committee has taken up the matter, reconsideration is no longer available. After that point, if the assembly wants the question back, it must use the motion to Discharge a Committee rather than reconsideration.

ref.quote: can be reconsidered if the committee has not begun consideration of the question

prompt: At a convention of the State Teachers Federation, a delegate moves to postpone a resolution to 11 a.m. tomorrow and make it a special order so it cannot be crowded out. Chair Robert Hale asks what vote that requires and why it differs from an ordinary postponement. Explain.
gold: In its simple form, Postpone to a Certain Time makes the question a general order and needs only a majority. But by a two-thirds vote a question can be postponed and made a special order, which gives it priority over general orders and the ability to interrupt pending business at the set time. The higher threshold applies because making it a special order suspends any rules that would interfere with taking it up at that hour.

ref.quote: By a two-thirds vote, however, a question can be postponed and made a special order, giving it priority over general orders as well as the ability to interrupt pending business.

prompt: A new member of the Cedar Ridge Rotary confuses Postpone to a Certain Time with Postpone Indefinitely. Chair Lucia Romano asks you to explain the difference between the two motions. Advise her.
gold: By Postpone to a Certain Time, action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event, so the question will come up again. Postpone Indefinitely, by contrast, does not actually postpone the question at all; it kills it for the session. So one defers a decision while the other suppresses the motion entirely.

ref.quote: action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour

prompt: At the Millbrook Homeowners Association, a motion to postpone a question to next month is pending, and a member wants to change the proposed date and also make it a special order. Chair Grace Kim asks in what respects the motion to Postpone may be amended. Explain.
gold: The motion to Postpone to a Certain Time is amendable as to the time to which the main question is to be postponed, and as to whether the postponed question is made a special order. Debate on it is limited and cannot go into the merits of the main question beyond what is necessary to decide whether and to when to postpone. An amendment merely proposing the special-order provision needs only a majority, even though adopting the amended motion would then require two-thirds.

ref.quote: Is amendable as to the time to which the main question is to be postponed

prompt: At the Fairhaven Arts Council, a member moves that debate on the pending resolution be limited to one three-minute speech per member. Chair Owen Barrett asks what vote is required to adopt this and why. Explain.
gold: The motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate requires a two-thirds vote. The higher threshold applies because the motion suspends the regular rules and because limiting debate takes away members' basic right to full discussion and can restrict a minority's ability to present its case. So a mere majority is not enough to impose the limit.

ref.quote: Requires a two-thirds vote—because it suspends the rules, and because limiting debate takes away the basic rights of all members to full discussion

prompt: At the Northgate Business League, a member has moved that debate be closed at 9 p.m., and another member wants both to debate that motion and to propose a different closing time. Chair Sandra Voss asks whether the motion to limit debate is debatable and whether it can be amended. Advise her.
gold: The motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is not debatable, so the chair should not open it to discussion. It is, however, amendable, but any such amendment, like the motion itself, is undebatable. So a member may move to change the proposed closing time, but that amendment is voted on without debate.

ref.quote: Is not debatable. 6. Is amendable, but any amendment, like the motion itself, is undebatable.

prompt: At the Elmwood Civic Club, a member moves the previous question and the chair is unsure whether a simple majority suffices. Chair Henry Cole asks you what vote the Previous Question requires and the reason for it. Explain.
gold: The Previous Question requires a two-thirds vote. If only a majority were required, a temporary majority of only one vote could deny the remaining members all opportunity to discuss a measure it wished to adopt or kill, so the higher threshold protects the right of debate. If the motion fails to reach two-thirds, debate simply continues as though it had not been made.

ref.quote: a temporary majority of only one vote could deny the remaining members all opportunity to discuss any measure

prompt: At the Southport Booster Club, a member moves the previous question, and another member rises to argue against closing debate while a third wants to amend the motion to apply to fewer questions. Chair Denise Park asks whether either is permissible. Advise her.
gold: The Previous Question is not debatable and is not amendable, so the chair should not allow argument on it and should put it to a vote at once. A member cannot amend it, though when a series of questions is pending a member may move the Previous Question again covering more or fewer of the pending questions before the first is voted on. Those competing motions are then voted on beginning with the one covering the most questions.

ref.quote: Is not debatable. 6. Is not amendable.

prompt: At the Kingsley Foundation board, the Previous Question has just been ordered on the immediately pending motion. Chair Victor Shaw asks exactly what effect that has on further debate, amendments, and other motions. Explain.
gold: Ordering the Previous Question immediately closes debate on, and stops amendment of, the immediately pending question and any other questions the order specified. It also prevents the making of any other subsidiary motions except the higher-ranking Lay on the Table. It does not, however, bar privileged or incidental motions, nor prevent a special order set for a particular hour from interrupting.

ref.quote: immediately closes debate on, and stops amendment of, the immediately pending question

prompt: During debate at the Willow Creek Choir, a member calls out 'I call for the question!' and the chair is tempted to simply stop debate. Chair Amara Diallo asks how she should treat that call. Advise her.
gold: A call such as 'I call for the question' or 'I move we vote now' is simply the motion for the Previous Question made in nonstandard form, and it is subject to all of the rules in this section. It therefore requires a second and a two-thirds vote, taken before the vote on the pending question, and cannot shut off debate on one member's say-so. If a member still wishes to speak and has not exhausted his right, the chair may not cut off debate merely on the call.

ref.quote: made in nonstandard form, and it is subject to all of the rules in this section

prompt: At the Ridgemont Association, the Previous Question has been ordered on a series consisting of a resolution, an amendment, and a motion to commit. Chair Paul Nguyen asks in what order the pending motions are now put to a vote. Explain.
gold: When the Previous Question is ordered on a series of motions, they are voted on in order of rank beginning with the immediately pending question, that is, in the reverse of the order in which they were made. So here the motion to Commit is voted on first, then the amendment, then the resolution. If the motion to Commit (or a Postpone) is adopted, further voting under the order stops.

ref.quote: they are voted on in order of rank beginning with the immediately pending question

prompt: At the Bayside Community Council, a member moves to 'table' a resolution she plainly dislikes, apparently hoping it will never return. Chair Felix Moreno asks whether the motion to Lay on the Table is proper for that purpose. Advise him.
gold: In ordinary assemblies the motion to Lay on the Table is not in order if the evident intent is to kill or avoid dealing with a measure; its legitimate purpose is only to set a question aside temporarily when something of immediate urgency arises. Using it to suppress a measure violates the rights of the minority and individual members. The chair should rule the motion out of order and note that killing the measure would require Postpone Indefinitely or a direct vote.

ref.quote: is not in order if the evident intent is to kill or avoid dealing with a measure

prompt: At the Glenwood Service Club, a member moves to lay the pending question on the table so the assembly can hear a departing speaker, and another member wants to debate and amend that motion. Chair Teresa Lund asks about its standard characteristics. Explain.
gold: The motion to Lay on the Table needs a second, is not debatable, is not amendable, and requires only a majority vote. It cannot be qualified in any way, so a member may not amend it or attach conditions to it. The chair should therefore put it to an immediate majority vote without debate.

ref.quote: Is not amendable. 7. Requires a majority vote.

prompt: At the Ashford Literary Society, a member moves to 'lay the question on the table until the next meeting.' Chair Gregory Hale asks whether that is a proper motion to Lay on the Table. Advise him.
gold: If a time for resuming consideration is specified in making the motion, it cannot be treated as Lay on the Table, which leaves open when or whether the question returns. Such a motion can be admitted only as a motion to Postpone to a Certain Time, which is debatable and amendable. So the chair should handle 'until the next meeting' as a motion to Postpone, not as laying on the table.

ref.quote: If a time for resuming consideration is specified in making the motion

prompt: At a convention of the Maplewood Federation, the time for a special order has passed but the chair has not taken it up. A single delegate rises and calls for the orders of the day. Chair Diane Foster asks whether a second and a vote are needed and what force the call has. Explain.
gold: A Call for the Orders of the Day requires no second and is not debatable, and it may be made even while another has the floor when a special order is being neglected. Upon a call by a single member the orders of the day must be enforced, except that a two-thirds vote can set them aside. So the chair must proceed to the special order unless the assembly, by two-thirds, votes not to.

ref.quote: the orders of the day must be enforced, except that a two-thirds vote can set them aside

prompt: At the Harborline Association, the orders of the day are called for, but the chair senses the assembly would rather keep discussing the current question. Chair Simon Blake asks how the assembly may decline to take up the orders of the day. Explain.
gold: The assembly by a two-thirds vote can set aside the orders of the day. The chair may put the question 'Will the assembly proceed to the orders of the day?', in which case two-thirds in the negative are needed to refuse. Alternatively a member may move, by a two-thirds affirmative vote, to extend the time for the pending question or to suspend the rules and take up another matter.

ref.quote: the assembly by a two-thirds vote can set aside the orders of the day

prompt: At the Crestview Association, a member interrupts pending business by rising to a question of privilege about disruptive noise. Chair Laura Simmons asks whether the assembly must vote on admitting it and how she should proceed. Advise her.
gold: Raising a question of privilege is ruled upon by the chair; no vote on the question's admissibility is taken unless the chair's ruling is appealed. The chair directs the member to state the matter, then rules whether it is genuinely a question of privilege and, if so, whether it is urgent enough to interrupt pending business. The device itself is undebatable and unamendable, though a main motion admitted as a question of privilege is then debatable and amendable.

ref.quote: Is ruled upon by the chair. No vote on the question’s admissibility is taken unless the chair’s ruling is appealed

prompt: A member of the Dunmore Society asks Chair Isaac Reed what kinds of matters can properly be raised as questions of privilege and which type has priority. Explain.
gold: Questions of privilege are of two types: those relating to the privileges of the assembly as a whole, and questions of personal privilege. The first may involve the assembly's organization, the comfort of members (heating, lighting, noise), the conduct of officers or guests, or the accuracy of published reports, and going into executive session is such a question. If the two types compete, questions of the privileges of the assembly take precedence over questions of personal privilege.

ref.quote: Questions of privilege are of two types: (1) those relating to the privileges of the assembly as a whole; and (2) questions of personal privilege.

prompt: At the Oakhaven Guild, while a question is pending a member moves to take a ten-minute recess to count ballots, and another member wants to change it to fifteen minutes. Chair Monica Reyes asks about the standard characteristics of this privileged motion. Explain.
gold: Made while business is pending, the privileged motion to Recess needs a second, is not debatable, and requires a majority vote. It is amendable as to the length of the recess, though any such amendment is undebatable. So the change to fifteen minutes may be proposed and is voted on without debate; note that a recess motion made while no question is pending would instead be a main motion.

ref.quote: Is amendable as to the length of the recess; any such amendment is undebatable.

prompt: At a regular monthly meeting of the Talbot Society, while a resolution is pending a member moves 'to adjourn,' and the chair is unsure whether debate or amendment is allowed. Chair Karen Boyd asks about the characteristics of the privileged motion to Adjourn. Explain.
gold: The adoption of any motion to adjourn closes the meeting immediately unless the motion specifies a later time for adjourning, in which case it is not privileged. As an unqualified privileged motion in an ordinary society with other regular meetings, it needs a second, is not debatable, is not amendable, requires a majority vote, and cannot be reconsidered. So the chair should put it to an immediate majority vote, though it may be renewed after progress in business or debate.

ref.quote: The adoption of any motion to adjourn closes the meeting immediately unless the motion specifies a later time for adjourning

prompt: At a convention of the Redwood Association, a delegate moves, while a question is pending, to set the time for an adjourned meeting to continue the session's business. Chair Nathan Weiss asks about this motion's rank and characteristics compared with the other privileged motions. Explain.
gold: The privileged motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn takes precedence over all other motions, ranking as the highest of the privileged motions, and it may even be moved after a vote to adjourn but before adjournment is declared. It requires a second and a majority vote, is not debatable, and can be reconsidered. It is amendable as to the date, hour, or place, though such amendments are undebatable; note it is privileged only while a question is pending.

ref.quote: Takes precedence over all other motions.

prompt: At the Lakeshore Garden Club, the chair states the question on a motion that was never seconded, and debate proceeds. Ten minutes into that debate, member Ruiz rises to a point of order that the motion was never seconded. Advise the chair on how to treat this point of order.
gold: The point of order is too late and should be ruled not well taken. As a general rule a point of order must be raised promptly at the moment the breach occurs; the time to object to a missing second was when the chair stated the question. Once debate on the motion has begun, the opportunity to raise that point has passed, no matter how clear the irregularity was. A point of order requires no second and is decided by the chair without debate.

ref.quote: The general rule is that if a question of order is to be raised, it must be raised promptly at the time the breach occurs.

prompt: Six weeks ago the Harborview Association adopted, by majority vote, a main motion that directly conflicts with a provision of its bylaws, and the motion is still being acted on. Member Cho asks whether it is too late to raise a point of order. Advise Cho.
gold: It is not too late. The usual timeliness rule does not apply to breaches of a continuing nature, where the action taken is null and void, and a point of order about such a breach can be made at any time during the continuance of the breach. A main motion that conflicts with the bylaws is one of these continuing breaches, so the point may be raised now regardless of how much time has elapsed. If the point is sustained, the conflicting action is declared null and void.

ref.quote: a point of order can be made at any time during the continuance of the breach

prompt: During a meeting of the Fairfield Booster Club, member Alvarez raises a point of order. A newer member asks you whether the point needs a second, whether the assembly debates it, and who decides it. Explain the handling of a point of order.
gold: A point of order does not require a second and is not debatable, though the chair may permit the member to explain the point and may hear knowledgeable members. It is normally ruled upon by the chair, and no vote is taken unless the chair is in doubt or the ruling is appealed. When the chair is in doubt, the chair may submit the question to the assembly for decision, and in that case it may become debatable under the rules that govern an appeal.

ref.quote: Is normally ruled upon by the chair. No vote is taken unless the chair is in doubt or his ruling is appealed.

prompt: At the Meridian Society, the chair rules a proposed amendment out of order as not germane. Member Blake believes the ruling is wrong. Explain what Blake may do, and what is required.
gold: Blake may appeal from the decision of the chair. Any two members can appeal: one member makes (or takes) the appeal and another seconds it, which takes the question from the chair and vests it in the assembly for final decision. An appeal must be seconded and must be made at the time of the ruling. Members have no right to criticize a ruling of the chair unless they appeal from it.

ref.quote: By one member making (or “taking”) the appeal and another seconding it, the question is taken from the chair and vested in the assembly for final decision.

prompt: An appeal has been taken and seconded at the Coastal Alliance. Member Deng asks what vote is needed to overturn the chair and whether the presiding officer, who is a member, may vote. Advise Deng.
gold: It takes a majority against the chair's decision to reverse it; a majority or a tie vote sustains the chair, on the principle that the chair's decision stands until reversed by a majority. Because a tie sustains the chair, a presiding officer who is a member may vote to create a tie and thereby sustain the ruling. An appeal is generally debatable, and when it is, no member may speak more than once except the chair, who may speak first and again at the close of debate. An appeal can be reconsidered.

ref.quote: A majority or a tie vote sustains the decision of the chair on the principle that the chair’s decision stands until reversed by a majority.

prompt: At the Ridgeway Council, member Farro asks the chair whether a certain motion would be in order, and the chair replies that it would not be. Farro wants to appeal that answer. Explain whether an appeal lies.
gold: No appeal can be made from the chair's response to a parliamentary inquiry or other query, because such a reply is an opinion, not a ruling on a question that has actually arisen. If Farro wishes to test the point, the correct course is to make the motion despite the chair's opinion; then, if the chair rules it out of order, Farro may appeal from that ruling. An appeal is applicable only to an actual ruling of the chair.

ref.quote: No appeal can be made from the chair’s response to a parliamentary inquiry or other query

prompt: At the Glenwood Foundation, the chair made a ruling several minutes ago, the assembly debated another matter, and now member Ito wants to appeal that earlier ruling. Advise the chair on timeliness.
gold: The appeal is too late. An appeal must be made at the time of the chair's ruling, and if any debate or business has intervened, it is too late to appeal. Because other business has come between the ruling and Ito's attempt, the ruling stands and no appeal is now in order.

ref.quote: If any debate or business has intervened, it is too late to appeal.

prompt: The Ashford Club has a special rule of order limiting each member to two minutes of debate. At a meeting, member Nunez wants to suspend that rule to allow longer speeches on an important question. Explain what vote is required and any limits.
gold: Suspending a rule of order such as this special rule requires a two-thirds vote; by whatever name a rule is called, if it relates to parliamentary procedure, its suspension requires a two-thirds vote. The motion must state its specific purpose, is not debatable, is not amendable, and must be seconded. It cannot be applied to the bylaws, and no rule protecting a minority of a particular size can be suspended over a negative vote as large as the minority protected.

ref.quote: if it relates to parliamentary procedure, it requires a two-thirds vote for its suspension.

prompt: At the Brookdale Society, a member proposes to suspend the rules to sidestep a requirement contained in the bylaws, arguing that nearly everyone present agrees. Advise the assembly whether this is possible.
gold: Rules in the bylaws (or constitution) cannot be suspended, no matter how large the vote in favor of doing so or how inconvenient the rule may be, unless the particular rule provides for its own suspension or is in the nature of a rule of order. So the assembly cannot suspend the rules to avoid this bylaw requirement even by a large or unanimous vote. If the requirement is unwanted, the proper remedy is to amend the bylaws under their amendment procedure, not to suspend them.

ref.quote: cannot be suspended—no matter how large the vote in favor of doing so or how inconvenient the rule in question may be

prompt: The Summit Association wants to suspend the rules so that absent members may cast votes by proxy on a pending question, and asks whether a unanimous vote would make this permissible. Advise the association.
gold: This cannot be done, even by a unanimous vote. Rules that embody fundamental principles of parliamentary law cannot be suspended, and it is a fundamental principle that voting is limited to members actually present when the vote is taken. Therefore the rules cannot be suspended so as to give the right to vote to a nonmember or to authorize absentee voting. Allowing such voting would require an authorization in the bylaws.

ref.quote: the rules cannot be suspended so as to give the right to vote to a nonmember, or to authorize absentee voting

prompt: At the Pinehurst Club a motion to suspend the rules for a particular purpose is voted down. Later in the same meeting member Ostrander wants to move it again, and also asks whether it could be renewed at a later meeting. Advise Ostrander.
gold: Once voted down, a motion to suspend the rules cannot be renewed for the same purpose at the same meeting, except by unanimous consent. It can, however, be renewed for the same purpose after an adjournment, even if the next meeting is held the same day, because the attendance or situation may have changed. Note also that any number of motions to suspend the rules for different purposes can be entertained at the same meeting.

ref.quote: It can, however, be renewed for the same purpose after an adjournment, even if the next meeting is held the same day.

prompt: The Westgate Society has an ordinary standing rule prohibiting the use of recording devices at meetings. Member Pell wants that rule suspended for one meeting so a session can be recorded. Explain the vote required.
gold: Because this is an ordinary standing rule that does not relate to parliamentary procedure, it can be suspended by a majority vote rather than a two-thirds vote. Through an incidental main motion adopted by a majority, such a standing rule can even be suspended for the duration of the current session. This lower threshold applies precisely because the rule concerns a matter like recording devices rather than parliamentary procedure.

ref.quote: Standing rules can be suspended by a majority vote.

prompt: At the Cedarbrook Club a controversial original main motion has just been made and debate has already started. Member Quill wants to keep the assembly from considering the motion at all. Explain whether an objection to the consideration of the question is available and what it takes.
gold: It is now too late. An objection to the consideration of a question can be raised only before there has been any debate or any subsidiary motion other than Lay on the Table; thereafter, consideration of the main question has begun and it is too late to object. Had it been timely, the objection would need no second, would not be debatable or amendable, and would require a two-thirds vote against consideration to be sustained. It can be applied only to original main motions.

ref.quote: thereafter, consideration of the main question has begun and it is too late to object.

prompt: At the Northfield Society an objection to the consideration of a main motion is sustained. Member Reyes asks what becomes of the motion and whether it can come up again. Advise Reyes.
gold: When an objection to consideration is sustained, the main motion is dismissed for that session and cannot be renewed during the same session except by unanimous consent or by reconsideration of the vote on the objection. Even so, the same main motion may be introduced again at any succeeding session, where it will be a new motion. If the objection had not been sustained, consideration of the motion would simply have proceeded as if no objection had been made.

ref.quote: the main motion is dismissed for that session and cannot be renewed during the same session except by unanimous consent or by reconsideration of the vote on the objection.

prompt: At the Stonebridge Club an objection to the consideration of a question was sustained, and member Tanaka now regrets it and wants the assembly to take up the motion after all. Explain what may be done.
gold: A vote sustaining an objection to consideration can be reconsidered. The motion to reconsider such a vote is undebatable and requires a majority vote for its adoption, and it can be taken up or called up only when no other motion is pending. If the reconsideration is adopted, the objection is presumed overturned, and the chair immediately states the question on the main motion without putting the objection to a vote again.

ref.quote: The motion to reconsider such a vote is undebatable and requires a majority vote for its adoption, and it can be taken up or called up only when no other motion is pending.

prompt: At the Vernon Association three independent resolutions on different subjects are offered together in one motion. Member Udall wants Resolution No. 3 voted on separately but does not have a majority behind him. Advise Udall on his rights.
gold: Udall does not need a majority or even a formal motion. When a series of independent resolutions on different subjects is offered in one motion, one or more of them must receive separate consideration and vote at the request of a single member, and the motion for Division of a Question is not used. Udall can simply demand a separate vote, for example by saying he calls for a separate vote on Resolution No. 3. This demand must be made before the question on adopting the series has actually been put to a vote.

ref.quote: one or more of the several resolutions must receive separate consideration and vote at the request of a single member

prompt: At the Whitman Club a single main motion contains several distinct parts, and member Vasquez moves the incidental motion for Division of a Question. A member asks whether that motion needs a second, whether it can be amended, and what vote decides it. Explain.
gold: The incidental motion for Division of a Question must be seconded and is not debatable. It is amendable, since another member may propose a different manner of dividing, and it requires a majority vote. It cannot be reconsidered. A motion may be divided only where each part can stand as a proper question on its own, and in practice the division is often arranged by unanimous consent.

ref.quote: Is amendable. 7. Requires a majority vote. 8. Cannot be reconsidered.

prompt: At the Oakmont Society the chair announces that a voice vote carried, but member Wren doubts the result was accurately judged. Wren asks whether a motion and a second are needed to have the vote retaken by rising. Advise Wren.
gold: No motion, second, or vote is needed. A call for a Division of the Assembly does not require a vote, since a single member can demand a division, and it may be called for without obtaining the floor, even when another has the floor. On such a demand the chair immediately retakes the vote by rising. It is in order from the moment the negative votes are cast until immediately after the result is announced; it is not debatable and cannot be reconsidered.

ref.quote: Does not require a vote, since a single member can demand a division.

prompt: At the Bayview Club a lopsided voice vote is plainly one-sided, yet member Yates keeps shouting Division after each such vote to slow the meeting down. Advise the chair on how to respond.
gold: The chair need not honor the demands. When it is clear that there has been a full vote and there can be no reasonable doubt as to which side is in the majority, a call for a division is dilatory. The chair should not allow the individual member's right to demand a division to be abused to the annoyance of the assembly, and may decline to retake the vote in such circumstances.

ref.quote: When it is clear that there has been a full vote and there can be no reasonable doubt as to which side is in the majority

prompt: At the Kingsley Society member Adler is unsure what motion to make and rises to a parliamentary inquiry. A member asks you what a parliamentary inquiry is and whether the chair must answer. Explain.
gold: A parliamentary inquiry is a question directed to the presiding officer to obtain information on parliamentary law or the organization's rules bearing on the business at hand. It is the chair's duty to answer such questions when it may assist a member to make an appropriate motion, raise a proper point of order, or understand the parliamentary situation or the effect of a motion. It requires no second, is not debatable, and no vote is taken. The chair is not obliged to answer hypothetical questions, and the chair's reply is an opinion, not a ruling, so it cannot be appealed.

ref.quote: It is the chair’s duty to answer such questions when it may assist a member to make an appropriate motion, raise a proper point of order, or understand the parliamentary situation or the effect of a motion.

prompt: At the Larkspur Association member Bishop wants to ask the treasurer about the current balance before a spending motion is decided. A member asks you how this differs from a parliamentary inquiry. Explain.
gold: This is a request for information, also called a point of information: a request directed to the chair, or through the chair to another officer or member, for information relevant to the business at hand but not related to parliamentary procedure. In contrast, a parliamentary inquiry concerns parliamentary law or the rules. Like a parliamentary inquiry, a request for information requires no second, is not debatable, and takes no vote; the exchange is conducted through the chair.

ref.quote: for information relevant to the business at hand but not related to parliamentary procedure.

prompt: At the Elmwood Club member Carter has just made a motion, but the chair has not yet stated it. Carter wants to withdraw it. A member asks whether the assembly's permission is needed. Advise Carter.
gold: No permission is needed at this stage. Before a motion has been stated by the chair, it is the property of its mover, who can withdraw it or modify it without asking the consent of anyone. In the brief interval between the making of the motion and the chair's stating it, Carter may simply withdraw or modify it. Permission of the assembly is required only after the chair has stated the motion as pending.

ref.quote: it is the property of its mover, who can withdraw it or modify it without asking the consent of anyone.

prompt: At the Fairmont Society a main motion has been stated by the chair, amended, and now has a subsidiary motion pending. Member Doyle, who made the main motion, wants to withdraw it. Explain whether Doyle can still do so and how.
gold: Doyle can still seek to withdraw it. A request for permission to withdraw a motion can be made at any time before voting on the question has begun, even though the motion has been amended and even though subsidiary or incidental motions may be pending. Because the motion has been stated by the chair, Doyle needs the assembly's permission; the chair first treats it as a unanimous-consent request, and if anyone objects, a majority vote decides. Any adhering motions cease to be before the assembly if the main motion is withdrawn.

ref.quote: can be made at any time before voting on the question has begun, even though the motion has been amended, and even though subsidiary or incidental motions may be pending.

prompt: At the Granville Club a motion was withdrawn earlier in the meeting. Member Ellis now wants to bring the same motion up again during that same meeting and asks whether the rule against renewing motions blocks it. Advise Ellis.
gold: The rule against renewal does not block it. After a motion has been withdrawn, the situation is as though it had never been made, so the same motion can be made again at the same meeting. The restrictions on renewing motions do not apply to a motion that was disposed of by being withdrawn, because the assembly was never actually called upon to decide it.

ref.quote: After a motion has been withdrawn, the situation is as though it had never been made; therefore, the same motion can be made again at the same meeting.

prompt: At the Halcyon Society, committee member Frost submits a written resignation during a meeting. Member Gray asks how the assembly handles this and whether the resignation takes effect on its own. Explain.
gold: A resignation is treated as a request to be excused from a duty: by submitting a resignation, the member is in effect requesting to be excused from a duty. The chair, on reading or announcing the resignation, can assume a motion that the resignation be accepted; the request to be excused is debatable and amendable and requires a majority vote, though it is often settled by unanimous consent. The resignation does not take effect on its own, and the member should not abandon the duties until the resignation has been accepted or there has been a reasonable opportunity for it to be accepted.

ref.quote: By submitting a resignation, the member is, in effect, requesting to be excused from a duty.

prompt: At the Ironwood Club a resolution was laid on the table earlier in the meeting. Member Hoffman wants to bring it back and asks who may do so, when, and what vote is required. Advise Hoffman.
gold: Any member may move to take the question from the table; it requires a second and a majority vote, and is not debatable or amendable. A question that has been laid on the table remains there and can be taken from the table during the same session, and also until the end of the next regular session if that session will be held before a quarterly time interval elapses. It cannot be moved while another question is pending, and it is in order only after some business has intervened since the question was laid on the table.

ref.quote: A question that has been laid on the table remains there and can be taken from the table during the same session

prompt: At the Juniper Society a resolution was laid on the table while an amendment and a motion to Commit were pending. When it is taken from the table, member Ives asks what is now before the assembly. Explain.
gold: When a question is taken from the table, it is before the assembly, with everything adhering to it, so far as possible, exactly as it was when laid on the table. So the amendment and the motion to Commit come back with it, and the immediately pending question is the motion to Commit, since that ranked highest of the pending motions. If the question is taken up on the same day, members who exhausted their debate cannot speak again; on a later day, debate rights are renewed.

ref.quote: it is before the assembly, with everything adhering to it, so far as possible (and with the exceptions noted below), exactly as it was when laid on the table.

prompt: At the Kenmore Association a policy adopted two years ago is now unpopular, and member J934 asks whether it is too late to rescind it and whether only those who voted for it originally may move to do so. Advise the member.
gold: It is not too late, and any member may move it. Unlike the motion to Reconsider, there is no time limit on moving to Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted after adoption of the measure, and these motions can be moved by any member regardless of how he voted on the original question. They can be applied to anything with continuing force and effect that resulted from a previously adopted main motion.

ref.quote: there is no time limit on making these motions after the adoption of the measure to which they are applied, and they can be moved by any member, regardless of how he voted on the original question.

prompt: At the Longmeadow Club member Keller wants to rescind a previously adopted ordinary resolution but has given no advance notice. Explain what vote will be required, and how notice would change it.
gold: To Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted, the assembly needs any one of the following: a two-thirds vote, a majority vote if notice stating the complete substance of the proposed change was given at the previous meeting within a quarterly interval or in the call of this meeting, or a vote of a majority of the entire membership. Because Keller gave no notice, a two-thirds vote or a majority of the entire membership is required. Had proper notice been given, a simple majority of those voting would suffice. These motions are debatable and can go into the merits of the action to be changed.

ref.quote: require (a) a two-thirds vote, (b) a majority vote when notice of intent to make the motion, stating the complete substance of the proposed change, has been given at the previous meeting within a quarterly time interval or in the call of the present meeting, or (c) a vote of a majority of the entire membership—any one of which will suffice.

prompt: At the Maplewood Society a motion adopted last month authorized a payment, and the check has already been cashed by the recipient. Member Larson wants to rescind that action. Advise whether it can be rescinded.
gold: It cannot be rescinded to the extent it has been carried out. Rescind and Amend Something Previously Adopted are not in order when something has been done, as a result of the vote, that is impossible to undo, such as a payment already made and received. Only the unexecuted part of an order can be rescinded or amended. Since the payment has been completed, that portion cannot be undone, though any part not yet carried out could still be rescinded.

ref.quote: When something has been done, as a result of the vote on the main motion, that is impossible to undo.

prompt: At the Norwood Club a motion needing a two-thirds vote failed on a 40-to-30 vote. Member Meade, who voted no, wants to move to reconsider, and a member questions whether Meade qualifies. Also explain the timing for making the motion. Advise.
gold: Meade qualifies. The motion to Reconsider can be made only by a member who voted with the prevailing side, and it is possible for a minority to be the prevailing side when a motion requiring a two-thirds vote is lost. Here the motion failed, so the prevailing side is those who voted no, and Meade voted no. As to timing, in an ordinary one-day meeting the motion to Reconsider must be made on the same day the vote was taken; in a session of more than one day, it may also be made on the next succeeding business-meeting day within the session.

ref.quote: It should be noted that it is possible for a minority to be the prevailing side if a motion requiring a two-thirds vote for adoption is lost.

prompt: At the Overton Society a vote that had required a two-thirds vote to pass is now the subject of a motion to Reconsider. Member Nolan asks what vote it takes to adopt the reconsideration and whether the question could be reconsidered again later. Advise Nolan.
gold: The motion to Reconsider requires only a majority vote, regardless of the vote necessary to adopt the motion being reconsidered, so a majority suffices even though the original motion needed two-thirds. As for doing it again, no question can be reconsidered twice unless it was materially amended during its first reconsideration. The motion to Reconsider itself cannot be reconsidered, and if it is lost it cannot be renewed except by unanimous consent.

ref.quote: By the same principle, no question can be reconsidered twice unless it was materially amended during its first reconsideration.

prompt: At the Fairview Garden Club, a new member asks President Alvarez to explain what a quorum actually measures, since only a handful of the people in the room ever vote on any given motion. Advise the president on how to explain the concept.
gold: A quorum is the number of members who must be present in order for business to be validly transacted. It is measured by the number of members present in the room, not by the number who happen to vote on any particular question. So the fact that only a few people vote on a motion does not affect whether a quorum exists; what matters is how many members are physically present.

ref.quote: The quorum refers to the number of members present, not to the number actually voting on a particular question.

prompt: The Lakeside Historical Society has an enrolled membership of 200 but its bylaws are silent on quorum. Treasurer Boyd asks Registered Parliamentarian Chen what quorum applies at their regular meetings. Explain the rule.
gold: When an ordinary deliberative assembly with an enrolled membership has bylaws that do not specify a quorum, the common parliamentary law sets the quorum at a majority of all the members. For the Lakeside society, that means 101 of the 200 members would have to be present. Because that is often impractical, societies are generally advised to adopt a smaller quorum in their bylaws.

ref.quote: In any other deliberative assembly with enrolled membership whose bylaws do not specify a quorum, the quorum is a majority of all the members.

prompt: The Riverton Association wants to lower its bylaw quorum from 50 members to 15. A member proposes to first delete the old quorum figure and then, at a later meeting, add the new one. Advise on why this is risky and what the correct method is.
gold: Deleting the quorum provision first is dangerous, because the moment the rule is struck out the quorum instantly reverts to a majority of the entire membership, and the society might then be unable to gather enough members to adopt the new figure. The correct procedure is to strike out the old provision and insert the new one as a single motion, voted on as one question. That way the society is never left without a workable quorum rule.

ref.quote: The proper procedure is to strike out the old provision and insert the new provision, which is moved and voted on as one question.

prompt: The nine-member Board of the Oakhill Foundation has no special quorum rule for board meetings, and its bylaws are silent on the point. Chair Delgado asks how many directors must be present to do business. Explain.
gold: For boards and committees, the default quorum is a majority of the members of that board or committee, unless a different quorum has been fixed by the bylaws or by the body that created the committee. With nine directors, a majority is five, so five directors must be present for the board to transact business. This differs from the assembly's own quorum rule, which does not automatically carry over to boards.

ref.quote: In all other committees and in boards, the quorum is a majority of the members of the board or committee unless a different quorum is provided for

prompt: At a poorly attended meeting of the Pinecrest Club, members went ahead and adopted several motions even though a quorum was clearly not present. Member Ruiz asks whether those actions stand. Advise on the effect.
gold: Business transacted in the absence of a quorum is null and void, apart from a few permitted procedural actions such as adjourning or taking steps to obtain a quorum. The motions the club adopted therefore have no legal effect. If the club still wants those actions, it must take them up again properly at a meeting where a quorum is present, or ratify them, though it is under no obligation to do so.

ref.quote: any business transacted (except for the procedural actions noted in the next paragraph) is null and void.

prompt: A regular meeting of the Westbrook League convenes but no quorum appears. President Nakamura wants to know what, if anything, the members present may legitimately do. Explain the permissible actions.
gold: Even without a quorum, the assembly may take a limited set of actions: it may fix the time to which to adjourn, adjourn, recess, or take measures to obtain a quorum.
