Related subsidiary and incidental motions, questions of privilege, and a Call for the Orders of the Day may also be handled if they bear on those actions or on conducting the meeting. What the members may not do is transact substantive business.

ref.quote: Even in the absence of a quorum, the assembly may fix the time to which to adjourn

prompt: At the Summit Society, only a few members show up, but they all agree they would like to approve an urgent contract 'by unanimous consent' since no one objects. Advise Secretary Ellis on whether this is permissible.
gold: The prohibition against transacting business without a quorum cannot be waived, even by unanimous consent, and even a valid notice cannot be given while no quorum is present. So the members cannot approve the contract simply because everyone present agrees. If the matter is urgent, the proper course is to fix the time for an adjourned meeting and then adjourn; any informal action taken now would be at the members' own risk and would require later ratification.

ref.quote: The prohibition against transacting business in the absence of a quorum cannot be waived even by unanimous consent

prompt: New president Okafor of the Bayside Council asks whether he must formally announce the headcount before starting each meeting. Advise him on his duty regarding the quorum at the opening of a meeting.
gold: Before calling the meeting to order, the presiding officer has a duty to satisfy himself that a quorum is present, but he need not announce the fact. If no quorum is present, he should wait a reasonable time to see whether one gathers. If it becomes clear that none will assemble, he calls the meeting to order, announces the absence of a quorum, and entertains a motion to adjourn or one of the other permitted motions.

ref.quote: Before the presiding officer calls a meeting to order, it is his duty to determine, although he need not announce, that a quorum is present.

prompt: Midway through a Hillcrest Club meeting several members leave, and member Tran wonders whether the pending debate is now invalid and how she may raise the issue. Advise on how continued quorum is treated once a meeting is underway.
gold: Once the chair has established a quorum and called the meeting to order, the continued presence of a quorum is presumed until the chair or a member notices its absence. Any member who notices the apparent loss of a quorum may raise a point of order, as long as she does not interrupt someone who is speaking. Debate already pending may continue until such a point is raised, and generally the point does not undo action already taken before it was raised.

ref.quote: the continued presence of a quorum is presumed unless the chair or a member notices that a quorum is no longer present.

prompt: The newly formed Cedar Valley Club adopts RONR as its authority but has no special order of business. Secretary Park asks Registered Parliamentarian Diaz what sequence its regular meetings should follow. List and explain the order.
gold: Under the standard order of business the headings are taken up in this sequence: reading and approval of the minutes; reports of officers, boards, and standing committees; reports of special committees; special orders; unfinished business and general orders; and finally new business. Because the club has adopted RONR and no special order of its own, this sequence is prescribed for its regular meetings. It fixes only the order of the headings, not the time devoted to each.

ref.quote: The customary or “standard” order of business comprises the following subdivisions:

prompt: At the Glenwood Society a member always moves 'that the minutes be approved,' and another insists this motion is required. Chair Salazar asks how the approval of minutes and any corrections are properly handled. Explain.
gold: A formal motion to approve the minutes is not necessary, though it is not out of order to make one. After the minutes are read, the chair asks whether there are any corrections and pauses. Corrections are usually handled by unanimous consent, but if a member objects to a proposed correction it is treated as a subsidiary motion to Amend under the usual rules. When there are no further corrections, the chair declares the minutes approved without any formal vote.

ref.quote: A formal motion to approve the minutes is not necessary, although such a motion is not out of order.

prompt: President Whitfield of the Ashford Club routinely opens the unfinished-business portion by asking, 'Is there any unfinished business?' Advise on whether this is correct and how the chair should instead proceed.
gold: The chair should not ask, 'Is there any unfinished business?' Instead, working from a memorandum prepared in advance, he should announce the heading only if the minutes show that business is due to come up, and then state the question on the first such item directly. When that item is disposed of, he proceeds through the remaining items in their proper order. Simply asking the room whether there is unfinished business is not the correct method.

ref.quote: but should state the question on the first item of business that is due to come up under this heading

prompt: At the Mapleton Association, members informally agreed during a meeting that a certain topic 'should be brought up next time.' At the next meeting the chair lists it under unfinished business. Advise whether that is correct.
gold: An informal understanding that a subject should be brought up at the next meeting does not make it unfinished business. A matter becomes unfinished business or a general order only through a formal process, such as being postponed, being pending at adjournment, or being set as an order of the day. Because none of those occurred here, the topic must be introduced at the next meeting as new business, not carried under the unfinished-business heading.

ref.quote: that a certain subject should be “brought up at the next meeting,” that does not make it unfinished business.

prompt: Members complain that Chair Bello of the Northgate Club rushes through the new-business portion so fast that no one can be recognized. Advise on how new business is properly conducted and the limits on the chair.
gold: After unfinished business and general orders are disposed of, the chair asks, 'Is there any new business?' and members may then introduce items or move to take matters from the table as they obtain the floor when nothing is pending. So long as members are reasonably prompt in claiming the floor, the chair cannot deprive them of the right to introduce legitimate business by hurrying through the proceedings. Rushing to close new business is therefore improper.

ref.quote: the chair cannot prevent the making of legitimate motions or deprive members of the right to introduce legitimate business, by hurrying through the proceedings.

prompt: The Brookdale Society does not fix its regular meeting dates in the bylaws; instead it sets each meeting's day and place by resolution. Secretary Ito asks whether notice must go out before every regular meeting. Explain.
gold: When an organization schedules the day, hour, or place of its regular meetings by resolution rather than fixing them in the bylaws, notice, also called the call of the meeting, must be sent to all members a reasonable time before each regular meeting. So Brookdale must send notice before every regular meeting. This differs from a society whose regular meeting dates are fixed in the bylaws, where such routine notice is not required.

ref.quote: notice (also referred to as the call of the meeting) must be sent to all members a reasonable time in advance of each regular meeting.

prompt: The Elmwood Foundation holds only one regular business meeting a year. Member Faulkner asks whether the organization must send notice even though the meeting date is fixed in the bylaws. Advise.
gold: Even where meeting dates are fixed in the bylaws, notice must be sent a reasonable time in advance of any regular meeting that is separated from the previous one by more than a quarterly time interval. Because the Elmwood Foundation's regular meetings are a year apart, notice is required before each one. Notice must likewise be sent before a convention of delegates.

ref.quote: notice must be sent a reasonable time in advance of each regular meeting that is separated by more than a quarterly time interval

prompt: The Vernon Club's bylaws require 'ten days' notice' of a meeting. Secretary Long asks how to count the ten days when the notice goes out and when the meeting falls. Explain the computation.
gold: Unless the bylaws provide otherwise, the required number of days is computed by counting all calendar days, including holidays and weekends. You include the day the notice is sent but exclude the day of the meeting itself. So for a ten-day requirement, count the day of mailing as day one and stop counting before the meeting day.

ref.quote: the number of days is computed by counting all calendar days (including holidays and weekends), excluding the day of the meeting but including the day the notice is sent.

prompt: The president of the Grantham Society wants to call a special meeting to deal with an urgent lease question. Advise on what the notice, or call, of that special meeting must contain.
gold: A special meeting is a separate session held apart from any regular meeting and convened only to consider business specified in its call. The notice must state the time, place, and purpose of the meeting, clearly and specifically describing the subject matter of the motions or items of business to be taken up, and it must be sent to all members a reasonable number of days in advance. So the Grantham notice must specifically describe the lease matter to be considered.

ref.quote: Notice of the time, place, and purpose of the meeting, clearly and specifically describing the subject matter of the motions or items of business to be brought up, must be sent to all members a reasonable number of days in advance.

prompt: At a special meeting of the Kingsley Club called to approve a budget, a member moves to also elect a new committee chair. Advise Chair Monroe on whether that motion may be entertained and what happens if it is adopted anyway.
gold: The only business that may be transacted at a special meeting is the business specified in the call, so a motion to elect a committee chair is out of order if that subject was not in the call. Privileged, subsidiary, and incidental motions arising in connection with the specified business may still be handled. If the assembly nevertheless takes action on unspecified business, that action is invalid unless later ratified at a regular meeting or another properly called special meeting.

ref.quote: The only business that can be transacted at a special meeting is that which has been specified in the call of the meeting.

prompt: The Danforth Society's special-meeting call describes the subject as 'amending the dues structure.' A member objects that the exact wording of every dues motion should have been in the call, and another asks whether such motions can be amended. Advise.
gold: The call of a special meeting must state its purpose, but it need not set out the exact wording of the individual motions to be considered. Stating the subject matter, such as amending the dues structure, is enough. And when a main motion related to business specified in the call is pending, it is as fully open to germane amendment as it would be at a regular meeting.

ref.quote: Although the call of a special meeting must state the purpose of the meeting, it need not give the exact content of individual motions that will be considered.

prompt: A group of members at the Trenton Club wants to convene a special meeting, but the bylaws say nothing about special meetings. Advise President Vega on whether a special meeting can properly be called.
gold: Special meetings can properly be called only when authorized in the bylaws, or, in the narrow context of disciplinary proceedings, when authorized by the assembly itself. Because the Trenton Club's bylaws contain no provision authorizing special meetings, one cannot properly be called. A bylaw authorizing special meetings should specify who may call them and the number of days' notice required.

ref.quote: Special meetings can properly be called only (a) as authorized in the bylaws

prompt: The secretary of the Harborview Society wants to include a summary of the lively debate and her own assessment of the arguments in the minutes. Advise on what the minutes of an ordinary society should and should not contain.
gold: In an ordinary society the minutes should record mainly what was done at the meeting, not what was said. They must never reflect the secretary's opinion, favorable or otherwise, on anything said or done. So the secretary should leave out summaries of the debate and any personal assessment, and simply record the motions and actions taken.

ref.quote: The minutes must never reflect the secretary’s opinion, favorable or otherwise, on anything said or done.

prompt: A new secretary at the Prescott Club asks Registered Parliamentarian Ford what belongs in the opening paragraph of the minutes. Explain what the first paragraph must contain.
gold: The first paragraph of the minutes should state the kind of meeting (regular, special, adjourned regular, or adjourned special); the name of the society; the date, time, and, if it varies, the place; whether the regular chairman and secretary were present or who substituted for them; and whether the minutes of the previous meeting were approved as read or as corrected. After that, the body of the minutes uses a separate paragraph for each subject, and the last paragraph gives the hour of adjournment.

ref.quote: the kind of meeting: regular, special, adjourned regular, or adjourned special

prompt: Two years after the fact, the Sherwood Club discovers a clear error in minutes that were long ago approved. Secretary Grant asks how such approved minutes may now be corrected. Advise.
gold: Minutes that have already been approved can still be corrected when an error or material omission is later established, even years afterward, by means of the motion to Amend Something Previously Adopted. That motion requires a two-thirds vote, or a majority vote with notice, or the vote of a majority of the entire membership, or unanimous consent. The original minutes should not be physically altered; instead the secretary may make a marginal notation referring to the correcting action.

ref.quote: which requires a two-thirds vote, or a majority vote with notice, or the vote of a majority of the entire membership, or unanimous consent.

prompt: At the Rockport Club a member moves to 'dispense with the reading of the minutes,' and another member thinks this means the minutes will never be read or approved. Advise Chair Hayes on what the motion actually does.
gold: A motion to dispense with the reading of the minutes only sets aside reading them for approval at the regular time; it is not a request to omit their reading altogether. It is adopted by majority vote without debate, and the minutes may then be taken up later in the meeting while no business is pending. If they are not taken up before adjournment, they are read and approved at the following meeting before the later minutes are considered.

ref.quote: A motion to “dispense with the reading of the minutes” is not a request to omit their reading altogether

prompt: The secretary of the Fairmont Society routinely records the names of both the mover and the seconder of every motion. Advise on whether both names belong in the minutes.
gold: The name of the member who makes a main motion should be entered in the minutes, but the name of the seconder should not be recorded unless the assembly orders it. So the secretary should stop routinely recording seconders' names. The maker's name is the proper entry.

ref.quote: The name of the maker of a main motion should be entered in the minutes, but the name of the seconder should not be entered unless ordered by the assembly.

prompt: After a close voice vote at the Larkspur Club, member Cortez shouts 'Division!' Some members insist the demand needs a second and a vote. Advise Chair Reyes on what a Division call is and how to respond.
gold: A Division of the Assembly is a demand that a voice vote or show-of-hands vote be retaken as a rising vote. Any single member may demand it without a second and without a vote of the assembly, so long as it is not used as a dilatory tactic when the result is clearly beyond doubt. When a member calls out 'Division,' the chair must immediately take the vote again by rising; no second or vote is needed.

ref.quote: any member (without a second) has the right to require that a voice vote (or even a vote by show of hands) be retaken as a rising vote, so long as he does not use the procedure as a dilatory tactic

prompt: After a division is taken at the Wexford Society, the rising vote still looks very close and members disagree about the outcome. Advise on how a counted vote may be obtained.
gold: A rising vote is not automatically counted, but either the chair on his own initiative or the assembly by majority vote can order it to be counted. If the chair does not order a count on a doubtfully close division, a member may, as soon as the result is announced, gain preference in recognition to move that the vote be counted. If that motion is seconded and adopted, the chair retakes the division as a counted rising vote.

ref.quote: Either the chair on his own initiative or the assembly by a majority vote can order such a vote to be counted.

prompt: Chair Abbott of the Kentfield Club takes a voice vote and genuinely cannot tell which side prevailed. She asks how she should proceed. Advise on handling an inconclusive vote about which the chair is in actual doubt.
gold: If the chair is in actual doubt about the result of a voice vote, she does not announce a result. She immediately retakes the vote, strictly speaking always as a rising vote. If the affirmative rising vote appears close enough to require a count, she should have the vote counted. If after an uncounted rising vote she still cannot determine the outcome, she must take the vote a third time as a counted rising vote.

ref.quote: he does not announce a result, but instead immediately retakes the vote—strictly speaking, always as a rising vote.

prompt: On a counted vote requiring a majority at the Sutton Club, the tally is 15 in favor and 15 against, not counting the presiding member, who is a voting member. Advise Chair Dobbs on his voting options and their effect.
gold: The presiding officer who is a member has the same right to vote as anyone else, but ordinarily exercises it only when his vote would affect the result. On a motion needing a majority, a tie without his vote means he can break it: he may vote in the affirmative to adopt the motion, or he may abstain, in which case the tie stands and the motion is lost. So with 15 to 15, voting yes carries the motion and abstaining defeats it.

ref.quote: When there is a tie without the chair’s vote, the chair can vote in the affirmative, and such a vote adopts the motion; but if the chair abstains from voting, the motion is lost.

prompt: President Iverson of the Marlow Club wants to speed through several routine, uncontroversial items. Explain how the device of unanimous consent works and how the chair phrases it.
gold: Unanimous consent lets the assembly dispose of routine or minor matters quickly, either by adopting a motion without stating the question and taking a formal vote, or by taking action without even a formal motion. The chair says something like, 'If there is no objection...' or asks, 'Is there any objection to...?' and then pauses. If no one objects, the action is agreed to; if any member objects, the chair must proceed to handle the matter by the regular procedure. Unanimous consent still requires the presence of a quorum.

ref.quote: the method of unanimous consent can be used either to adopt a motion without the steps of stating the question and putting the motion to a formal vote, or it can be used to take action without even the formality of a motion.

prompt: At a Carlton Society meeting the assembly has drifted past the hour set for a scheduled item, and member Pratt wants to force the assembly back on schedule. Advise on the motion available and its effect.
gold: A member may use a Call for the Orders of the Day, a privileged motion, to require the assembly to conform to its agenda or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due at the time. On such a call the chair must proceed to the proper order of business unless two-thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise. So Pratt can call for the orders of the day and compel the assembly back to the scheduled item unless a two-thirds vote sets the call aside.

ref.quote: a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time

prompt: At the Lakeside Garden Club, a main motion is put to a rising vote and 19 members cast a vote. The presiding officer, Nadia, asks you how many affirmative votes are needed to adopt the motion, and what "majority vote" means when the bylaws prescribe nothing special. Explain.
gold: Unless a rule provides otherwise, the basic requirement to adopt an ordinary motion is a majority vote, which means more than half of the votes cast by persons entitled to vote, excluding blanks and abstentions. With 19 votes cast, more than half of 19 is more than 9 and a half, so a majority is 10. If 10 or more members vote in the affirmative, the motion is adopted.

ref.quote: the basic requirement for approval of an action or choice by a deliberative assembly

prompt: A 12-member board of a nonprofit meets; one member is absent, so 11 are present. On a main motion the count is 5 in favor, 4 against, and 2 members abstain. The chair, Marcus, asks whether the motion is adopted. Advise him.
gold: A majority vote means more than half of the votes actually cast, excluding blanks and abstentions; absent members and abstainers are simply not counted. Here only 9 votes were cast (5 plus 4), and more than half of 9 is 5, so the 5 affirmative votes are a majority. The motion is adopted, because the 2 abstentions and the 1 absent member have no bearing on whether a majority of the votes cast was obtained.

ref.quote: more than half of the votes cast by persons entitled to vote, excluding blanks or abstentions

prompt: The Riverton Historical Society is voting on a motion to Limit Debate, which requires a two-thirds vote. Exactly 30 members cast votes. The secretary, Priya, asks you how many affirmative votes are needed and what an unqualified "two-thirds vote" means. Explain.
gold: An unqualified two-thirds vote means at least two thirds of the votes cast by persons entitled to vote, excluding blanks and abstentions. Two thirds of 30 votes cast is 20, so at least 20 affirmative votes are required. If fewer than 20 vote in the affirmative, the motion fails, regardless of how many members are present or abstain.

ref.quote: at least two thirds of the votes cast by persons entitled to vote, excluding blanks or abstentions

prompt: During an election meeting of the Ashwood Association, a member moves to close nominations while another member still clearly wishes to nominate someone. The chair, Dolores, wants to know why this motion cannot pass on a simple majority. Explain the principle.
gold: A two-thirds vote is required to adopt any motion that closes nominations or the polls, or otherwise limits the freedom of nominating or voting. This higher threshold exists as a compromise protecting the rights of the individual against the assembly, since cutting off nominations restricts members' basic freedom of choice. The motion to close nominations therefore needs a two-thirds vote, and it should not be used to shut out a member who still wishes to nominate.

ref.quote: closes nominations or the polls, or otherwise limits the freedom of nominating or voting

prompt: The Fairview Society has a total membership of 150 and a quorum of 10. At a meeting 30 members are present, and 25 of them participate in a counted vote. A member asks the parliamentarian how many votes constitute "a majority of the entire membership" for this body. Answer.
gold: A majority of the entire membership is more than half of the total number of members of the voting body, not merely of those present or voting. With a total membership of 150, a majority of the entire membership is 76. It makes no difference that only 30 were present or that only 25 voted; that basis is measured against the whole 150-member roll.

ref.quote: at a meeting of a society with a total membership of 150 and a quorum of 10, there are 30 members present

prompt: The bylaws of the Meridian Club require certain actions to pass by "a majority of the members present." A member, Theo, plans to abstain to stay neutral and asks whether his abstention truly counts as neutral. Explain the effect and why such a basis is generally discouraged.
gold: When the voting basis is a majority of the members present, an abstention has the same practical effect as a negative vote, because the affirmative must exceed half of everyone present rather than half of those actually voting. So Theo cannot remain genuinely neutral by abstaining; his silence works against the motion. For this reason bases counted on the members present are generally undesirable, since they deny members the right to abstain, and the chair must count those present immediately after the affirmative vote is taken.

ref.quote: Since an abstention in such cases has the same effect as a negative vote

prompt: The Oakhurst Society has both a general membership and a separate executive board. At a board meeting, a motion requires "a majority of the entire membership." The board chair, Wanda, asks whether that means a majority of the whole society or a majority of the board. Advise her.
gold: A majority of the entire membership means a majority of the total number of those who are members of the voting body at the time of the vote. Since the meeting is a board meeting, the voting body is the board, so it means a majority of the board's membership, not of the society at large. The requirement is measured against the board's roster, and any board vacancies do not enlarge or reduce that count beyond the current membership.

ref.quote: a majority of the total number of those who are members of the voting body at the time of the vote

prompt: In an election at the Brookline Guild with three candidates, Candidate A gets 40 votes, B gets 35, and C gets 25. Someone claims A is elected as the top vote-getter. The chair, Ismael, asks whether a plurality elects. Explain.
gold: A plurality is simply the largest number of votes among three or more choices, and a plurality that is not a majority never elects anyone unless a special rule adopted in advance (for officers, a bylaw) so provides. Here A has a plurality but not a majority, since 40 out of 100 is not more than half. Absent a bylaw allowing a plurality to elect, no one is elected and the assembly must ballot again until a candidate obtains a majority.

ref.quote: A plurality that is not a majority never chooses a proposition or elects anyone to office except by virtue of a special rule previously adopted

prompt: A main motion at the Cedar Council results in a tie, 20 to 20, and the presiding officer, Grace, is a voting member who has not yet voted. She asks what happens on a tie and whether she may still vote. Advise her.
gold: On a tie vote, a motion requiring a majority is lost, because a tie is not a majority. Since Grace is a member and the vote is not by ballot, she is not obliged to vote but may vote whenever her vote will affect the result. She could vote in the affirmative to make it 21 to 20 and adopt the motion; she cannot, however, vote twice, once as a member and again as presiding officer.

ref.quote: On a tie vote, a motion requiring a majority vote for adoption is lost, since a tie is not a majority

prompt: A member appeals a ruling of the chair at the Northgate Association. The vote on sustaining the chair is a tie, and the chair, Renard, cast the vote that produced the tie. A member argues the appeal must therefore succeed. Explain the correct result.
gold: On an appeal, a tie vote sustains the decision of the chair, even when the chair's own vote created the tie. The principle is that the chair's ruling can be reversed only by a majority against it, and a tie is not a majority. Because the vote is tied, the chair's decision stands and the appeal fails.

ref.quote: a tie vote sustains the chair’s decision, even though his vote created the tie

prompt: At a convention, a delegate named Sofia was selected to represent two different constituent chapters. She asks whether she may cast two votes on a question, one for each chapter she represents. Advise her.
gold: It is a fundamental principle of parliamentary law that each member of a deliberative assembly is entitled to one, and only one, vote on a question. This holds even where a person is chosen by more than one body, so a delegate selected by two constituent chapters may cast only a single vote. An individual's right to vote also cannot be transferred to another person, such as by proxy.

ref.quote: each person who is a member of a deliberative assembly is entitled to one—and only one—vote on a question

prompt: At the Palmetto Society, the chair wants to require every member present to vote on a pending question, and separately a member is filling three committee seats but wishes to vote for only two candidates. The chair, Bianca, asks whether members can be forced to vote and whether partial abstention is allowed. Explain.
gold: Although a member with an opinion has a duty to vote, he can abstain, because no one can be compelled to vote. The chair therefore cannot require members present to cast a vote. When a member is entitled to vote for several positions, such as multiple committee seats, he may also partially abstain by voting for fewer than all of those for whom he could vote.

ref.quote: he can abstain, since he cannot be compelled to vote

prompt: A motion at the Summit Club proposes to award a service contract to a firm of which a member, Harold, is an officer and from which he would profit personally. The chair asks whether Harold must be barred from voting on the motion. Advise the chair.
gold: No member should vote on a question in which he has a direct personal or pecuniary interest not common to the other members, so Harold ought to abstain on a motion from which he would personally profit. However, this is a matter of propriety, not enforceable exclusion: no member can be compelled to refrain from voting in such circumstances. The chair may not prohibit Harold from voting, though the expectation is that he will voluntarily abstain.

ref.quote: However, no member can be compelled to refrain from voting in such circumstances

prompt: At the Crestwood League, a member named Julia is a candidate for president and is embarrassed to vote for herself. Someone tells her the rule against voting on a matter of personal interest forbids it. The chair asks you to clarify. Explain.
gold: The rule against voting on a question of direct personal interest does not mean a member cannot vote for herself for an office or position for which members generally are eligible. Julia may properly vote for herself in the election. If members could never vote on questions affecting themselves, an organization could not, for instance, vote to hold a banquet, and a small minority could improperly force charges against the majority.

ref.quote: a member should not vote for himself for an office or other position to which members generally are eligible

prompt: During a voice vote at the Harborview Society, a member named Owen wants to switch his vote. In one case he asks before the chair announces the result; in another, a secret ballot has already been counted. The chair asks how each is handled. Explain.
gold: Except when the vote was taken by ballot or another secret method, a member has the right to change his vote up to the time the result is announced. After the result is announced, he may change it only by unanimous consent of the assembly, requested and granted without debate immediately after the announcement. Where the vote was by secret ballot, however, there is no right to change a vote in this way, since doing so would reveal how the member voted.

ref.quote: a member has a right to change his vote up to the time the result is announced

prompt: After a voice vote at the Glenwood Club, a member, Priscilla, is unsure of the result and calls out "Division." She then insists that the chair produce an exact count. The chair asks you what a Division entitles a single member to require. Explain.
gold: By demanding a Division, any single member can require an inconclusive voice vote (or a show-of-hands vote) to be retaken as a rising vote. However, no individual member can compel the vote to be counted; a count requires either the chair's own decision or a majority vote ordering it. So Priscilla's demand forces a standing vote, but to obtain an exact tally she would have to move that the vote be counted and have that motion carry.

ref.quote: no individual member can compel it to be counted

prompt: The bylaws of the Fernbrook Society require that officers be elected by ballot. With only one candidate for president and everyone in agreement, a member moves to dispense with the ballot and elect by acclamation. The chair, Gideon, asks whether the assembly may do so. Advise him.
gold: When the bylaws require a vote to be taken by ballot, that requirement cannot be suspended, even by a unanimous vote, so as to take the vote by a nonsecret method. The assembly cannot elect by acclamation in place of the required ballot. A ballot must be taken even for the single candidate, and members retain the right to write in other eligible persons.

ref.quote: this requirement cannot be suspended—even by a unanimous vote

prompt: At the Willow Creek Club, the presiding officer, Anita, who is a member, forgot to cast her ballot during a ballot election, and the polls have now closed. She asks whether she may still vote or whether she may only vote to break a tie. Advise her.
gold: When the vote is by ballot, the presiding officer who is a member can always vote at the same time as the other members, just like anyone else, rather than being limited to breaking ties. But if she fails to vote before the polls are closed, she cannot then vote without the permission of the assembly. Since the polls have closed, Anita would need the assembly's leave to cast her ballot now.

ref.quote: The presiding officer, if a member of the assembly, can always vote in the case of a ballot at the time other members do

prompt: In a ballot vote at the Stonebridge Guild, several folded slips turn out to be completely blank. A teller, Malik, asks whether these blanks count toward the number of votes cast and thus raise the majority needed. Explain.
gold: Blank ballots and other ballots that show no preference are ignored by the tellers and treated as abstentions. They are not counted as votes cast and therefore do not affect the number needed for a majority. Members sometimes submit blanks precisely to conceal that they did not wish to vote, but such slips have no effect on the result.

ref.quote: the tellers ignore blank ballots and other ballots that indicate no preference, treating them as abstentions

prompt: At the Lindenwood Society only one treasurer is to be elected, but one ballot has two different names written in for treasurer. The chairman of tellers, Rosa, asks how to handle it and whether it counts toward the total. Explain.
gold: A ballot that names too many candidates for a single office is counted as one illegal vote cast for that office, because the tellers cannot tell which candidate the voter actually preferred. It is treated as a vote cast, so it is included in the total for computing the majority, but it is not credited to either named candidate. Thus the double-marked ballot raises the number of votes cast without helping either treasurer candidate.

ref.quote: a ballot that contains votes for too many candidates for a given office is counted as one illegal vote cast for that office

prompt: During a ballot count at the Maplewood Association, several ballots spell the candidate's name "Jonson" instead of "Johnson," and there is only one such candidate. A teller wants to discard them as illegal. The chair asks you whether that is correct. Advise.
gold: Technical errors such as a misspelled name do not make a ballot illegal so long as the meaning of the ballot is clear. Since there is only one candidate to whom "Jonson" could refer, those ballots plainly indicate that candidate and must be credited to him, not discarded. Only when the meaning of a ballot is genuinely doubtful, and it could affect the result, must the tellers refer it to the assembly to decide.

ref.quote: Technical errors, like the misspelling of a word or name, do not make a vote illegal if the meaning of the ballot is clear

prompt: After a close ballot election at the Ridgeway Society, it emerges that some unidentifiable ballots may have been cast by nonmembers, and the number involved is large enough that it could have changed the outcome. The chair, Bernard, asks what must be done. Advise him.
gold: If there is evidence that unidentifiable ballots were cast by persons not entitled to vote, and there is any possibility those ballots could affect the result, the entire ballot vote is null and void and a new ballot vote must be taken. Because the tainted ballots here could have changed the close outcome, the election cannot stand. The society must hold the balloting over again. (Ballots identifiable as cast by nonmembers would instead simply be excluded from the count.)

ref.quote: the entire ballot vote is null and void, and a new ballot vote must be taken

prompt: In a ballot election at the Elmhurst Society, 97 votes are cast: Miller 51, Wilson 24, Strong 14, plus 7 for an ineligible person and 1 ballot rejected as two folded together. A member asks how many votes were needed and whether Miller is elected. Explain.
gold: All ballots indicating a preference, including the illegal votes, count as votes cast, so the total is 97 and a majority necessary for election is 49. Miller received 51, which exceeds 49, so Miller is elected. The 7 votes for the ineligible person and the folded-together ballot are still counted in the 97 for purposes of computing the majority, but they are not credited to any valid candidate.

ref.quote: Number of votes cast……97 Necessary for election (majority)……49

prompt: At the Bayview Club, a teller drafting the report wants to list how many members were eligible to vote and how many abstained, and then have the tellers announce who won. The chair, Cordelia, asks whether that is proper. Explain.
gold: The tellers' report does not include the number of members eligible to vote or the number abstaining; it reports only the votes cast, the number needed, and the votes for each candidate or side, including any illegal votes. Moreover, the reporting teller never declares the result: the tellers read their report and hand it to the chair, and the chair alone reads it again and declares the outcome. So the extra figures should be left off, and the chair, not the tellers, announces who is elected.

ref.quote: The tellers’ report does not include the number of members eligible to vote nor the number abstaining

prompt: The Hillcrest Society, a small local club whose members answer to no outside constituency, is considering taking a controversial vote by roll call so everyone's position is on record. The chair, Foster, asks whether a roll-call vote is appropriate here. Advise him.
gold: A roll-call vote records how each member votes, so it has exactly the opposite effect of a ballot vote. It is properly used in representative bodies whose proceedings are published, so that constituents can see how their representatives voted. In an ordinary local society whose members are not responsible to a constituency, a roll call is generally out of place and a motion to take one is usually dilatory.

ref.quote: it has exactly the opposite effect of a ballot vote

prompt: A member of the Delmar Association who will miss the next meeting asks whether he can mail in his vote on a pending motion, or have the count combine in-person votes with mailed ballots. The bylaws are silent on absentee voting. Advise him.
gold: It is a fundamental principle of parliamentary law that the right to vote is limited to members actually present when the vote is taken, and exceptions such as voting by mail or proxy must be expressly provided in the bylaws. Since the bylaws are silent, the member cannot vote by mail on this motion. Moreover, an organization should never mix the votes of those present with ballots mailed in by absentees, because the absentees cannot adjust to debate or amendments and would effectively be voting on a different question.

ref.quote: the right to vote is limited to the members of an organization who are actually present at the time the vote is taken

prompt: A member of the unincorporated Sycamore Society wants to authorize a friend to vote in his place at the meeting by signing a proxy. The bylaws say nothing about proxies. The chair, Delia, asks whether proxy voting is allowed. Advise her.
gold: Proxy voting is not permitted in ordinary deliberative assemblies unless the state's law requires it or the charter or bylaws provide for it. Because this society's bylaws are silent and it is an ordinary voluntary association, proxy voting is not allowed, and the friend cannot vote in the absent member's place. Proxy voting is incompatible with the deliberative assembly, where membership and the right to vote are individual, personal, and nontransferable.

ref.quote: Proxy voting is not permitted in ordinary deliberative assemblies unless the laws of the state in which the society is incorporated require it, or the charter or bylaws of the organization provide for it

prompt: At the Kingsley Society, the only nominee for secretary has just withdrawn, and a member worries that with no nominees left, a ballot election cannot proceed. The chair, Amara, asks whether nominations are strictly required before a ballot election. Explain.
gold: Strictly speaking, nominations are not necessary when an election is by ballot or roll call, because each member is free to vote for any eligible person, whether or not nominated. Members are always free to write in, on a ballot, the name of an eligible person who has not been nominated. So the election can proceed by ballot even with no standing nominee, though in practice nominations are useful to avoid repeated balloting before someone gains a majority.

ref.quote: members are always free to “write in,” on a ballot, the name of an eligible person who has not been nominated

prompt: At the Rockford Club, the president proposes to appoint the nominating committee himself and to serve on it ex officio, noting he appoints all other committees. A member objects. The chair asks you whether this is proper. Explain.
gold: The nominating committee should be elected by the organization or its executive board, and in an organized society the president should neither appoint this committee nor be a member of it, ex officio or otherwise. Even where bylaws make the president an ex-officio member of, or the appointer of, all committees, the nominating committee should be expressly excepted. So the president's proposal is improper; he should not appoint or sit on the nominating committee.

ref.quote: the president should not appoint this committee or be a member of it—ex officio or otherwise

prompt: At the Westfield Association's election, nominations for vice-president were closed, but a member now realizes a strong candidate was overlooked and wants to nominate her. The chair, Lorenzo, asks whether nominations can be reopened and what vote is needed. Advise him.
gold: After nominations have been closed, they can be reopened by a majority vote. So a member may move to reopen nominations, and if that motion carries by majority, the overlooked candidate can be nominated. This contrasts with the motion to close nominations, which requires a two-thirds vote because it limits the freedom of nominating.

ref.quote: After nominations have been closed, they can be reopened by a majority vote

prompt: In a three-way ballot election at the Norwood Society, no candidate reaches a majority on the first ballot. A member moves to drop the lowest candidate from the next ballot to force a decision. The chair, Estelle, asks whether that is required and how repeated balloting works. Explain.
gold: When no candidate attains a majority, the balloting is simply repeated for that office as many times as necessary to obtain a majority for a single candidate. Individuals are never removed from candidacy on the next ballot unless they voluntarily withdraw, which they are not obligated to do. So the lowest candidate cannot be dropped against his will; the low candidate may even become a compromise "dark horse," and the society should keep balloting until someone gets a majority.

ref.quote: the balloting is repeated for that office as many times as necessary to obtain a majority vote for a single candidate

prompt: At the Ellsworth Club's election, one winner was present and said nothing, while another winner was absent and had never consented to being a candidate. The chair, Rupert, asks when each election becomes final and whether the vote can later be reconsidered. Explain.
gold: An election becomes final immediately if the candidate is present and does not decline, or if absent but has consented to the candidacy. So the present winner's election is final at once. The absent winner who never consented is not elected finally until notified, and it becomes final then only if he does not immediately decline; if he declines, another vote may be taken. Once an election has become final in this way, it is too late to reconsider the vote.

ref.quote: An election to an office becomes final immediately if the candidate is present and does not decline

prompt: At the Lakeshore Civic League's annual meeting, President Alvarez has engaged Diane Okonkwo as parliamentarian. During a disputed point of order, a member insists that the parliamentarian herself must decide whether the motion is in order. Explain the parliamentarian's proper role during a meeting and why she does not decide the question.
gold: The parliamentarian is a consultant who advises the president, officers, and members on matters of parliamentary procedure. During a meeting her role is purely advisory and consultative. Parliamentary law gives the chair alone the power to rule on questions of order or to answer parliamentary inquiries, so Okonkwo does not decide the point of order herself. She may quietly advise President Alvarez, but the ruling belongs to the chair.

ref.quote: The parliamentarian’s role during a meeting is purely an advisory and consultative one

prompt: During a meeting of the Hartwell Foundation board, parliamentarian George Liu advises the chair that a proposed amendment is not germane, but the chair, after hearing the advice, rules the amendment germane anyway. A member protests that the chair must follow the parliamentarian. Advise the assembly on who makes the final ruling and what recourse the parliamentarian has.
gold: After the parliamentarian expresses an opinion on a point, the chair has the duty to make the final ruling and has the right either to follow the parliamentarian's advice or to disregard it. So the chair here is entitled to rule as he did; the parliamentarian's opinion does not bind him. If, however, the parliamentarian's advice on important procedural issues is habitually disregarded, he may find it necessary at the end of the engagement or session to resign.

ref.quote: the chair has the duty to make the final ruling and, in doing so, has the right to follow the advice of the parliamentarian or to disregard it

prompt: Nadia Farrell, a member of the Greenfield Association, has agreed to serve as its parliamentarian for the year. She asks whether she may still debate and vote on motions as she used to. Explain the impartiality duty of a member who serves as parliamentarian.
gold: A member who acts as parliamentarian has the same duty as the presiding officer to maintain a position of impartiality. Accordingly, she does not make motions, participate in debate, or vote on any question except in the case of a ballot vote. If a member feels she cannot properly forgo these rights, she should not accept the position. Farrell may vote only when the vote is by ballot; otherwise she must refrain.

ref.quote: does not make motions, participate in debate, or vote on any question except in the case of a ballot vote

prompt: On a rising vote at the Milbrook Society, the count is tied and the parliamentarian, who is a member, realizes his single vote by ballot would break the tie. He asks whether he may cast a deciding vote as the presiding officer sometimes does. Advise him.
gold: No. A member serving as parliamentarian does not cast a deciding vote, even if his vote would affect the result, because doing so would interfere with the chair's prerogative of casting a deciding vote. The parliamentarian may vote only in the case of a ballot vote, and even then he may not use his vote to decide a tie. Breaking the tie, if the chair chooses, is the presiding officer's prerogative, not the parliamentarian's.

ref.quote: He does not cast a deciding vote, even if his vote would affect the result, since that would interfere with the chair’s prerogative of doing so

prompt: During debate on a motion he feels strongly about, member-parliamentarian Tomas Reyes proposes to step aside as parliamentarian just long enough to speak and vote on that one motion, the way a president relinquishes the chair to debate. Explain whether this is permissible.
gold: It is not permissible. Unlike the presiding officer, who may temporarily relinquish the chair to take part in debate on a particular motion, the parliamentarian cannot temporarily relinquish his position in order to exercise such rights on a particular motion. As parliamentarian, Reyes must maintain impartiality throughout and may not make motions, debate, or vote (except on a ballot vote). If he cannot forgo those rights, he should not have accepted the position.

ref.quote: the parliamentarian cannot temporarily relinquish his position in order to exercise such rights on a particular motion

prompt: At a meeting of the Cedar Ridge Club, parliamentarian Priya Menon notices the chair has skipped a required step that affects a member's substantive rights, but the chair has not asked her anything. She wonders whether she should stay silent unless consulted. Explain the parliamentarian's duty here and how she should act.
gold: During a meeting the parliamentarian's work is limited to giving advice to the chair and, when requested, to any other member. But it is also her duty, as inconspicuously as possible, to call the chair's attention to any error in the proceedings that may affect the substantive rights of any member or otherwise do harm. So Menon should not wait to be asked; she should quietly and discreetly alert the chair to the error rather than stay silent.

ref.quote: It is also the duty of the parliamentarian— as inconspicuously as possible—to call the attention of the chair to any error in the proceedings

prompt: The chair of the Ashford Guild tells the parliamentarian, Mr. Coleman, to speak up only when directly asked, since he does not want frequent interruptions. Explain why an experienced parliamentarian should not wait to be asked, and what arrangement should exist between the two.
gold: In advising the chair, the parliamentarian should not wait until asked for advice, because that may be too late. There should be an understanding that occasions will arise when the chair must listen to the parliamentarian's suggestions, even if it means momentarily not giving full attention to others or asking the assembly to stand at ease during the consultation. An experienced parliamentarian will often see a problem developing and head it off with a few words to the chair, keeping the chair informed and able to act at the correct time.

ref.quote: the parliamentarian should not wait until asked for advice—that may be too late

prompt: During a heated meeting, the chair repeatedly invites the parliamentarian to stand and explain procedural points directly to the whole assembly. A member asks whether this is the proper way for the parliamentarian to operate. Explain when a parliamentarian should address the assembly directly.
gold: Only on the most involved matters should the parliamentarian actually be called upon to speak to the assembly, and even that practice should be avoided if at all possible. The parliamentarian's advice normally goes privately to the chair, not to the whole body. Having the parliamentarian routinely address the assembly is improper; the chair should instead receive the advice quietly and then act or rule himself.

ref.quote: Only on the most involved matters should the parliamentarian actually be called upon to speak to the assembly; and the practice should be avoided if at all possible

prompt: A convention planning committee asks where the parliamentarian should sit and how visibly the presiding officer should rely on her. Explain the proper seating and the caution the chair should observe.
gold: The parliamentarian should be assigned a seat next to the chair, so as to be convenient for consultation in a low voice. At the same time, the chair should try to avoid checking with the parliamentarian too frequently or too obviously. Frequent or conspicuous consultation can undermine members' confidence, so the chair should consult discreetly and rely on advance preparation to minimize the need for consultation during the meeting.

ref.quote: The parliamentarian should be assigned a seat next to the chair, so as to be convenient for consultation in a low voice

prompt: President Whitfield engages a parliamentarian only to sit beside her during the meeting and never discusses the agenda beforehand. Explain the parliamentarian's proper duties in relation to the president and when the main work should be done.
gold: The president, knowing in advance the business to come before the assembly, should confer with the parliamentarian before the meetings open, and during recesses, to anticipate problems and to avoid, as much as possible, frequent consultation during the meetings. The parliamentarian's main work should be done outside the meeting. President Whitfield should meet with the parliamentarian ahead of time rather than treat her as a mere in-meeting fixture.

ref.quote: should confer with the parliamentarian before the meetings open, and during recesses, in order to anticipate any problems

prompt: The board of the Riverside Association wants to dictate exactly which parliamentarian the incoming president must use. Explain who properly selects the parliamentarian and what role the board or society has regarding the parliamentarian's fee.
gold: If a parliamentarian is needed, the president should be free to appoint one in whom he has confidence. The board or society, however, must approve any fee that will be required. So the board should not force a particular parliamentarian on the president; selection is the president's, while approval of the fee rests with the board or society. When needed for only one meeting, the parliamentarian should be appointed well in advance, since the main work is done outside the meeting.

ref.quote: the president should be free to appoint one in whom he has confidence. The board or society must approve any fee

prompt: A national organization is planning its convention and assumes it can hire a parliamentarian a day or two before the opening session. Explain the parliamentarian's proper role in convention preparation and when engagement should occur.
gold: A key consultant in preparing for a convention should be the parliamentarian, who should be engaged well in advance, and ideally a seasoned professional. Although the parliamentarian gives advice at convention meetings, seated next to the presiding officer, the most important work may be performed before the convention opens. During preparation the parliamentarian serves as principal adviser to the president, officers, and committee chairmen, and the Credentials, Standing Rules, Program, Resolutions, and other committees should consult with him. Hiring only a day or two beforehand defeats this function.

ref.quote: A key consultant in the preparation for a convention should be the parliamentarian, *who should be engaged well in* *advance*

prompt: A society is nominating a president and some members argue the position should go to its most popular fundraiser regardless of parliamentary skill. Explain what qualities a presiding officer should have and whether having a parliamentarian removes that need.
gold: The presiding officer, especially of a large assembly, should be chosen principally for the ability to preside. This person should be well versed in parliamentary law and thoroughly familiar with the bylaws and other rules of the organization, even if a parliamentarian will assist. Having a parliamentarian does not excuse the chair from this competence. Any presiding officer should also remember that no rules can take the place of tact and common sense on the part of the chairman.

ref.quote: The presiding officer of an assembly—especially of a large one—should be chosen principally for the ability to preside

prompt: A newly elected chair of the Fairview Council is unsure what her core duties are when handling motions. Explain the presiding officer's principal duties in bringing questions before the assembly and disposing of them.
gold: It is the duty of the presiding officer to state and to put to vote all questions that legitimately come before the assembly as motions or that otherwise arise, and to announce the result of each vote. If a motion is not in order, the chair rules that it is not in order, though she may instead suggest an in-order alternative the maker agrees to offer. The chair also opens the meeting, announces business in proper sequence, recognizes members entitled to the floor, and enforces the rules of debate and decorum.

ref.quote: To state and to put to vote all questions that legitimately come before the assembly

prompt: At the Oak Street Club, a member makes and another seconds a motion, and the maker immediately begins debating it before the chair says anything. The chair is unsure whether the motion is properly before the assembly. Explain the step the chair must perform.
gold: Neither the making nor the seconding of a motion places it before the assembly; only the chair can do that, by stating the question. Until the chair states the question, the motion is not pending and is not open to debate. So the chair must first state the question on the motion; only then does it become pending and may the maker speak first in debate if he wishes.

ref.quote: Neither the making nor the seconding of a motion places it before the assembly; only the chair can do that

prompt: As the chair of the Delaney Society puts a motion to a vote, he misstates its wording. A member wants to correct it. Explain how the chair should put the question and what may be done about an erroneous wording.
gold: When debate appears closed, the chair puts the question after once more making clear the exact question the assembly must decide, and where confusion is possible he should ensure members understand the effect of an aye and a no vote. If the chair's wording of the question is erroneous, a point of order may be made until any member has actually voted. Except as corrected in response to such a point of order, the exact wording the chair uses is definitive, and the minutes must match it.

ref.quote: If the chair’s wording of the question is erroneous, a point of order may be made until any member has actually voted

prompt: The chair of the Brookdale Guild takes a voice vote, hears a strong chorus of ayes, and immediately declares the motion adopted without calling for the noes. A member objects. Explain the chair's duty in calling for the vote, and the treatment of abstentions.
gold: The chair must always call for the negative vote, no matter how nearly unanimous the affirmative vote may appear; the only common relaxation is for noncontroversial complimentary or courtesy motions, and even then any member's objection requires the negative to be called. So the chair here erred by skipping the noes. The chair does not call for abstentions, since the number responding is meaningless; to abstain means simply not to vote at all.

ref.quote: The chair must always call for the negative vote, no matter how nearly unanimous the affirmative vote may appear

prompt: During debate, the chair of the Northgate Association, an expert on the topic, wants to interrupt a speaker to correct what he considers a factual mistake. Explain whether the chair may do so and the limits on interrupting a speaker.
gold: The chair may not interrupt on that basis. The presiding officer cannot interrupt a person who has the floor so long as that person does not violate the assembly's rules and no disorder arises, and the chair must never interrupt a speaker simply because he knows more about the matter than the speaker does. Except in committees and small boards, the chair also does not enter into discussion of the merits of pending questions. He should let the speaker finish.

ref.quote: The presiding officer must never interrupt a speaker simply because he knows more about the matter than the speaker does

prompt: Impatient to move on, the chair of the Sumner Club announces that debate is closed while a member who has not yet spoken is still seeking recognition. Explain whether the chair has this power.
gold: The chair does not have that power. The presiding officer cannot close debate so long as any member who has not exhausted his right to debate desires the floor. Debate can be closed only by order of the assembly, which requires a two-thirds vote. So the chair may not unilaterally cut off the member seeking the floor; the assembly must vote to close debate.

ref.quote: The presiding officer cannot close debate so long as any member who has not exhausted his right to debate desires the floor

prompt: The president of the Kingsley League feels strongly about a pending main motion and wants to argue for it from the chair. Explain whether he may debate while presiding and what he must do if he wishes to participate.
gold: Although the chair, if a member, has the same rights in debate as anyone, the impartiality required of the chair precludes his exercising those rights while presiding; normally he should say nothing on the merits of pending questions. If, on a rare occasion, he believes he must participate, he must relinquish the chair, turning it over to a qualified vice-president or member who has not spoken. He then may not return to the chair until the pending main question is disposed of, since he has shown himself a partisan on that matter.

ref.quote: the impartiality required of the chair in an assembly precludes his exercising these rights while he is presiding

prompt: A member appeals the chair's ruling, and the chair wants to explain his reasoning. He asks whether he must step down from the chair to speak, as he would to debate an ordinary motion. Advise him.
gold: He need not step down. In debate on an appeal, or on a point of order the chair has submitted to the assembly's judgment, the rule requiring the chair to relinquish the chair to participate does not apply, and the presiding officer does not leave the chair, because his participation relates to the function of presiding. He may speak in defense of his ruling from the chair, and indeed may speak first in preference to other members and again at the close of debate.

ref.quote: the foregoing rule does not apply, and the presiding officer does not leave the chair, since his participation in the debate relates to the function of presiding

prompt: A motion is made to censure the president of the Ellisville Society for his conduct. The president intends to preside over the debate on that motion. Explain the proper handling when a motion refers to the presiding officer himself.
gold: When a motion refers only to the presiding officer in a capacity not shared with other members, or commends or censures him, he should turn the chair over to the vice-president or appropriate temporary occupant during the assembly's consideration of that motion, just as he would to take part in debate. So the president should relinquish the chair for the censure motion. He should not, however, hesitate to put a question on electing officers or appointing a committee even if he is included.

ref.quote: he should turn the chair over to the vice-president or appropriate temporary occupant

prompt: At the Parkview Association, when a committee presents its report, the committee's chairman is allowed to take over the podium and put the question on adopting the report to a vote. Explain why this practice is improper.
gold: Permitting a committee chairman to preside over the assembly or put questions to a vote during presentation and consideration of the committee's report violates numerous principles of parliamentary law relating to the chair's appearance of impartiality and the inappropriateness of the chair entering into debate, and it disregards the regular presiding officer's duty to preside. The regular presiding officer should retain the chair and put the question; the committee chairman advocates for the report as a partisan and should not preside over its consideration.

ref.quote: violates numerous principles of parliamentary law relating to the chair’s appearance of impartiality

prompt: During debate at the Harmon Club, a member once addresses another member directly instead of speaking through the chair. The chair asks how firmly he should respond to such a minor lapse. Explain the proper handling of a slight breach of order.
gold: For only a slight breach of order, such as addressing another member instead of the chair, or in a single instance failing to confine remarks to the merits of the pending question, the chair simply raps lightly, points out the fault, and advises the member to avoid it. The member can then continue speaking if he commits no further breaches. More formal procedures are reserved for serious offenses; a minor lapse calls only for this gentle correction.

ref.quote: the chair simply raps lightly, points out the fault, and advises the member to avoid it

prompt: A member of the Weston Society repeatedly questions the motives of other members by name during debate despite a warning. Explain how the chair calls the member to order and what happens if the offender had the floor.
gold: For a more serious offense, such as repeatedly questioning others' motives by name, the chair normally warns the member first, but with or without warning the chair or any member can call the member to order. If the chair does so, he says the member is out of order and will be seated; if a member makes the call, the chair, finding the point well taken, declares the offender out of order and directs him to be seated. If the offender had the floor, the chair clearly states the breach and puts to the assembly the undebatable question, 'Shall the member be allowed to continue speaking?'

ref.quote: the chair clearly states the breach involved and puts the question to the assembly: “Shall the member be allowed to continue speaking?”

prompt: A member of the Danforth League persists in grave, disorderly conduct after repeated warnings. The chair asks what 'naming' the offender means and how seriously it should be treated. Explain, including what the chair directs beforehand.
gold: In cases of obstinate or grave breach of order, the chair can, after repeated warnings, 'name' the offender, which amounts to preferring charges and should be resorted to only in extreme circumstances. Before taking such action, when it begins to appear it may become necessary, the chair directs the secretary to take down the objectionable or disorderly words used. That direction, and the words taken down, are entered in the minutes only if the chair finds it necessary to name the offender.

ref.quote: the chair can, after repeated warnings, “name” the offender, which amounts to preferring charges

prompt: After naming a disruptive member at the Colby Association, the chair wants to expel him from the hall on his own authority. Explain who has the power to impose a penalty and whether a formal trial is needed for an offense committed in the meeting.
gold: The chair has no authority to impose a penalty or to order the offending member removed from the hall; the assembly has that power. So the chair cannot expel the member on his own; the assembly must decide the penalty. For an offense against the assembly occurring in a meeting, no formal trial is needed provided any penalty is imposed promptly after the breach, since the witnesses are all present and make up the body that determines the penalty.

ref.quote: Although the chair has no authority to impose a penalty or to order the offending member removed from the hall, the assembly has that power

prompt: During debate at the Sherwood Guild, one member calls another a 'liar' and says his statement 'is false.' The chair asks how he should handle personal attacks in debate. Explain the rule and the chair's duty.
gold: In debate a member may condemn the nature or likely consequences of a measure in strong terms, but must avoid personalities and can under no circumstances attack or question another member's motives; the measure, not the member, is the subject of debate. A member cannot say another's statement 'is false,' though he might say he believes there is strong evidence the member is mistaken. The moment the chair hears words such as 'fraud,' 'liar,' or 'lie' used about a member, he must act immediately and decisively to correct the matter and prevent its repetition.

ref.quote: under no circumstances can he attack or question the motives of another member. The measure, not the member, is the subject of debate

prompt: A candidate teaching a class on debate is asked why members are not allowed to argue back and forth with each other directly, and how one member may ask a speaker a question. Explain the rule on addressing remarks.
gold: Members cannot address one another directly; they must address all remarks through the chair. This keeps debate impersonal and orderly. If, while a member is speaking, another wishes to ask him a question, the questioner rises and addresses the chair, proceeding as under a Request for Information; the speaker may permit the question or not, and if he does, the time is taken out of his own. Directing remarks through the chair rather than at each other reduces friction and personal confrontation.

ref.quote: Members of an assembly cannot address one another directly, but must address all remarks through the chair

prompt: A member rises to a parliamentary inquiry asking whether it is in order to move the previous question. The chair wonders whether he must answer, and whether he must answer purely hypothetical questions. Explain the chair's duty regarding parliamentary inquiries.
gold: A parliamentary inquiry is a question directed to the presiding officer to obtain information on parliamentary law or the organization's rules bearing on the business at hand. It is the chair's duty to answer such questions when doing so may assist a member to make an appropriate motion, raise a proper point of order, or understand the parliamentary situation or the effect of a motion. The chair is not, however, obliged to answer hypothetical questions. So he should answer this practical inquiry about the previous question.

ref.quote: It is the chair’s duty to answer such questions when it may assist a member to make an appropriate motion, raise a proper point of order, or understand the parliamentary situation

prompt: The Fairhaven Public Library Foundation's executive board includes, as an ex-officio member, the mayor of the city, who is not a member, officer, or employee of the Foundation. At a board meeting Director Alvarez objects that the mayor should not be allowed to make motions or vote because 'he isn't really one of us.' Advise the board on what rights this ex-officio member actually has.
gold: An ex-officio board member who is not under the authority of the society still enjoys the full privileges of board membership, including the right to make motions and to vote. Such a member simply carries none of the obligations of membership, meaning there is no duty to attend or participate. The mayor may therefore make motions and vote just like any other director, so Director Alvarez's objection should not be sustained.

ref.quote: he has all the privileges of board membership, including the right to make motions and to vote, but none of the obligations

prompt: At the Lakeside Choral Society, the bylaws make the society's president an ex-officio member of the executive board and also provide that the society's president shall serve as the board's presiding officer. Secretary Nguyen asks whether the president should be counted when determining whether a board quorum is present. Explain the correct treatment.
gold: When an ex-officio board member is also ex-officio an officer of the board, that person has the obligation to serve as a regular working member and is therefore counted in the quorum. The president here is not the kind of outside ex-officio member who may be disregarded for quorum purposes. So the president should be included both in the board's membership and in figuring whether a quorum is present.

ref.quote: he of course has the obligation to serve as a regular working member and is therefore counted in the quorum

prompt: Ms. Okafor sits on the board of the Riverbend Athletic Club solely because she chairs the club's Membership Committee, a chairmanship the bylaws make an ex-officio board seat. She has just resigned the committee chairmanship but insists she may keep serving out the year on the board. Advise the club.
gold: An ex-officio seat exists only by virtue of the office or chairmanship that entitles the person to it. When a member ceases to hold that qualifying office, the ex-officio board membership terminates automatically. Because Ms. Okafor has given up the committee chairmanship, her seat on the board ended at that moment and she cannot continue in it.

ref.quote: his membership on the board terminates automatically

prompt: The seven-member board of the Cedar Grove Neighborhood Association meets around a conference table. A new director, accustomed to large conventions, keeps insisting that every motion be formally seconded and that speakers stand and be recognized before speaking. Advise the chair on how the rules differ for a board this small.
gold: In a board meeting with not more than about a dozen members present, the small-board rules relax much of the formality required in a large assembly. Among other things, motions need not be seconded, members may remain seated while making motions or speaking, and there is no limit on how many times a member may speak to a debatable question. The chair may therefore proceed informally, and the new director's insistence on full formality is not required for a board of seven.

ref.quote: Motions need not be seconded

prompt: Between meetings of the Hillcrest Museum board, President Diaz telephones each of the nine directors individually, and all nine agree to spend $5,000 on emergency roof repairs. Diaz now claims 'the board has approved' the expenditure. Advise whether that is a valid board action.
gold: A board can act only at a regular or properly called meeting at which a quorum is present, because the members must have the opportunity to deliberate together as a body. Approval gathered separately by telephone, individual interviews, or in writing, even from every single member, is not the approval of the board. Unless the bylaws authorize electronic meetings, the roof expenditure has not been validly approved and should be taken up at an actual meeting.

ref.quote: The personal approval of a proposed action obtained separately by telephone, by individual interviews, or in writing, even from every member of the board, is not the approval of the board

prompt: The general membership of the Summit Hiking Club voted to hold its banquet at the Grand Hotel. Afterward the club's executive board, thinking it a better deal, voted to move the banquet to a different venue. Members are asking whether the board could override the membership's choice. Advise them.
gold: An executive board acts for the society only within the powers delegated to it, and no action of the board can alter or conflict with a decision already made by the assembly of the society. Any such conflicting board action is null and void. The board therefore could not overturn the membership's vote on the banquet venue; if the board wishes to change it, the matter must go back to the assembly, which alone can rescind or amend its own decision.

ref.quote: no action of the board can alter or conflict with any decision made by the assembly of the society, and any such action of the board is null and void

prompt: A newly elected director of the Greenfield Arts Council assumes the board can decide anything it likes about the organization's affairs. The council's parliamentarian is asked to explain the actual source and limits of the board's authority. Advise.
gold: A society has no executive board, and its officers cannot act as a board, except as the bylaws provide. Once established, the board has only such power as is delegated to it by the bylaws or by a vote of the society's assembly referring particular matters to it. The board is not free to act on whatever it pleases; its authority is bounded by what the bylaws grant and by any matters the membership specifically refers to it.

ref.quote: the board has only such power as is delegated to it by the bylaws or by vote of the society’s assembly referring individual matters to it

prompt: The board of the Westbrook Foundation is elected in classes, with one-third chosen each year for three-year terms. At the end of one year, several matters had been introduced but only tabled, not finally disposed of. A director asks whether those unfinished matters simply carry over to the incoming board. Advise.
gold: Where a fixed portion of a board is chosen periodically, the body becomes, in effect, a new board each time such a group takes up membership. Consequently, when the outgoing portion leaves, matters that were only temporarily but not finally disposed of fall to the ground, except those still in the hands of a committee to which they were referred.
