The tabled matters do not automatically carry over and would have to be introduced anew before the reconstituted board.

ref.quote: it becomes, in effect, a new board each time such a group assumes board membership

prompt: During a heated meeting of the Oakdale Historical Society's executive board, one director repeatedly interrupts and shouts down others. Some members want the board to censure or suspend him on the spot. Advise the board on the strongest penalty it may itself impose on a disorderly board member at the meeting.
gold: A board may protect itself against a disorderly member during its meetings, but the maximum penalty the board itself may impose is to require the offender to leave the meeting room for the remainder of that meeting. The board cannot, on its own, censure, suspend, or otherwise discipline the member beyond that. Any greater penalty would have to be pursued through the disciplinary processes of the society, not decided by the board at the meeting.

ref.quote: the maximum penalty which may be imposed upon a disorderly member of the board is that he be required to leave the meeting room during the remainder of the meeting

prompt: Members of the Pinewood Garden Club have asked to see their executive board's minutes. The board is willing but is unsure what vote it needs, given that some past meetings were ordinary and open. Advise the board on the vote required to let members view minutes that are not protected by executive-session secrecy.
gold: Ordinarily only board members may examine the board's minute book, but the board may order that specified persons, including all society members, be permitted to view or receive copies. A motion to do so is an incidental main motion, and if the minutes are not protected by executive-session secrecy it can be adopted by a majority vote. The board may therefore open the ordinary minutes to members by a simple majority; a higher threshold would apply only to minutes protected by executive-session secrecy.

ref.quote: A motion to do so is an incidental main motion, which can be adopted by a majority vote if the minutes are not protected by executive-session secrecy

prompt: The membership of the Bayview Yacht Club authorized a special committee and left the manner of appointment to be decided by majority vote, then delegated the selection to Commodore Reyes. One appointee has now resigned, and there is dispute over who may name the chairman and fill the vacant seat. Advise.
gold: The power to appoint a committee carries with it the power to appoint the committee's chairman and to fill any vacancy that later arises in it. Because the appointing power in this case was placed in Commodore Reyes, that same power extends to designating the chairman and to filling the seat left by the resignation. No separate authorization from the membership is needed for the commodore to do so.

ref.quote: The power to appoint a committee carries with it the power to appoint the chairman and to fill any vacancy that may arise in the committee

prompt: A member of the Ashford Scholarship Committee, which was appointed by the club president acting alone under bylaw authority, wants to resign. She is unsure to whom she should submit her resignation and who then fills her seat. Advise her.
gold: A committee member's resignation should be addressed to the appointing power, and it is that power's responsibility to fill the resulting vacancy. Since the president appointed this committee, the resignation should go to the president, who is then responsible for naming a replacement. The member does not submit her resignation to the committee itself or to the general membership.

ref.quote: The resignation of a member of a committee should be addressed to the appointing power, and it is the responsibility of that power to fill the resulting vacancy

prompt: The Maplewood Civic League has a standing Finance Committee charged with all budget matters. At a meeting, a member moves to create a special committee to draft next year's budget because he distrusts the Finance Committee. Advise the chair on whether such a special committee may be appointed.
gold: A special committee may not be appointed to perform a task that already falls within the assigned function of an existing standing committee. Because drafting the budget lies within the Finance Committee's established province, the proposed special committee would improperly encroach on it. If the members are dissatisfied, the remedy is to instruct or reconstitute the standing committee, not to appoint a special committee to duplicate its work.

ref.quote: A special committee may not be appointed to perform a task that falls within the assigned function of an existing standing committee

prompt: Last spring the Elmwood Alumni Association appointed a special committee to plan its centennial celebration. The association has since held its annual meeting, and a member argues the special committee automatically expired at that meeting. Advise whether the committee still exists.
gold: A special committee is appointed for a specific purpose and continues to exist until its task is accomplished and it submits its final report, unless it is discharged sooner. The mere fact that an annual meeting intervenes does not discharge a special committee. The centennial committee therefore remains in existence and continues its work until it reports finally or is expressly discharged.

ref.quote: The fact that an annual meeting intervenes does not discharge a special committee

prompt: The Northgate Association wants to create a standing committee that would have continuing authority to act for the society on all public-relations matters without needing specific instructions each time. The bylaws are silent, so a member proposes to establish it by an ordinary majority vote at the next meeting. Advise on what is required.
gold: A standing committee that is to have standing authority to act for the society on a class of matters without specific instructions must be constituted either by a provision in the bylaws or by a resolution amounting to a special rule of order. Such a resolution requires either previous notice and a two-thirds vote or a vote of a majority of the entire membership. A simple majority vote without notice will not suffice to give the committee that continuing authority.

ref.quote: either previous notice and a two-thirds vote or a vote of a majority of the entire membership

prompt: The bylaws of the Crestline Society make the president ex officio a member of all committees except the Nominating Committee. At a three-person committee meeting, only two of the three regular members are present, but the president also attends. A member asks whether the president's presence helps establish a quorum. Advise.
gold: When the bylaws make the president an ex-officio member of committees, the president has the right, but not the obligation, to participate, and is not counted in determining the number required for a quorum or whether a quorum is present. The president's attendance therefore does not count toward the committee's quorum. Whether a quorum exists is decided by the regular committee members alone, and the president may participate but adds nothing to the quorum figure.

ref.quote: the president is an ex-officio member who has the right, but not the obligation, to participate in the proceedings of the committees, and he is not counted in determining the number required for a quorum or whether a quorum is present at a meeting

prompt: The Harborview Club appointed a seven-member special committee but said nothing about its quorum. At a meeting attended by three members, the chair proposes to transact business. A member questions whether three is enough. Advise on the committee's quorum.
gold: Unless the assembly has prescribed a different quorum, the quorum in a committee is a majority of its membership. For a seven-member committee that means at least four members must be present. With only three present there is no quorum, so the committee cannot properly transact business at that meeting.

ref.quote: The quorum in a committee is a majority of its membership unless the assembly has prescribed a different quorum

prompt: A special committee of the Delmar Association is scattered across the country and finds it genuinely impractical to hold a meeting, yet its report is due. Every member individually agrees on the recommendations. The chair asks whether the committee may still submit a report reflecting that agreement. Advise.
gold: As a rule, a report may contain only what was agreed to by a majority at a properly called meeting with a quorum present. For committees, however, there is an exception: if it is impractical to bring the members together for a meeting, the report may contain what has been agreed to by every one of its members. Since unanimous individual agreement exists and a meeting is genuinely impractical, the committee may submit the report on that basis.

ref.quote: if it is impractical to bring its members together for a meeting, the report of the committee can contain what has been agreed to by every one of its members

prompt: The president of the Rockland Guild, without any authorization in the bylaws or from the membership, announces that she has appointed several committees and assigned them tasks. A member questions whether she had the authority to do this. Advise.
gold: Appointment of committees by the president is a valid method only when that power has been given to the president, either by the bylaws or by action of the assembly in the particular case. A president cannot simply assume the power to appoint committees. Because the Rockland Guild's president had no such authorization, her unilateral appointments were not proper, and the method of appointment must instead be decided by the assembly.

ref.quote: The president cannot assume such power, however, unless it is given to him by the bylaws or by action of the assembly in the individual case

prompt: The bylaws of the Stanton Society contain no article prescribing how they may be amended. A member wants to know how the society can nonetheless amend them. Advise on the vote and notice required.
gold: Even when the bylaws are silent on amendment, they are not unamendable. In that situation they can be amended by a two-thirds vote if previous notice has been given, or, as an alternative, they can be amended by the vote of a majority of the entire membership. Either route is sufficient; the society simply cannot amend them by a bare majority of those present without notice.

ref.quote: they can be amended by the vote of a majority of the entire membership

prompt: The Brookfield Club is drafting its amendment article and proposes to require that a proposed amendment be submitted at 'a previous meeting.' The parliamentarian suggests this wording is unwise. Explain why 'the previous meeting' is preferable to 'a previous meeting.'
gold: The notice requirement should specify submission at 'the' previous meeting rather than 'a' previous meeting. Using 'a' would permit indefinite delay before the amendment is taken up, which would defeat the object of giving notice, namely to alert members so that all who are interested can arrange to be present when it is considered. The club should therefore word its article to require submission at the previous meeting.

ref.quote: since the latter would permit indefinite delay and would defeat the object of giving notice

prompt: The bylaws of the Vernon Association set annual dues at $10, and notice was given of an amendment to strike $10 and insert $25. At the meeting, a member moves to amend the pending amendment to make the dues $40 instead. Advise the chair on whether that secondary amendment is in order.
gold: When previous notice of a bylaw amendment has been given, no amendment to that amendment is in order if it increases the modification of the provision being amended, so as to prevent members from taking advantage of those who stayed away relying on the noticed change. An amendment to change the $25 figure to any amount between $10 and $25 would be in order, but raising it to $40, which exceeds the noticed change, is out of order, even by unanimous consent. The chair should rule the motion for $40 not in order.

ref.quote: no amendment to a bylaw amendment is in order that increases the modification of the article or provision to be amended

prompt: A properly authorized committee of the Fairview Society has submitted a complete revision of the bylaws. During consideration, a member argues that instead of adopting the revision, the assembly should just amend the existing bylaws here and there to keep the old document with a few changes. Advise on what the assembly may and may not do with a revision pending.
gold: A revision is a new document open to amendment as fully as if the society were adopting bylaws for the first time, so the assembly is not confined to the committee's points of change and may perfect it by amendments. But because the old bylaws are not pending, the assembly's choices are to adopt the revision as perfected or to reject it and leave the old bylaws intact. The old document cannot be altered with a view to retaining it in a changed form, so the member's proposed approach is not available.

ref.quote: the old document cannot be altered with a view to retaining it in a changed form

prompt: The Kingsley Club received notice of a proposed substitute for Article V of its bylaws. During debate a member tries to amend a sentence within the substitute that is word-for-word identical to the current bylaws and was not among the changes noticed. Advise the chair.
gold: When a substitute for a section or article is proposed, only changes within the scope of the noticed substitute may be considered. Portions of the substitute that remain the same as in the existing version cannot be amended, because no notice of proposed change was given as to those areas. The chair should rule out of order any attempt to amend the unchanged sentence.

ref.quote: Portions of the substitute which remain as in the existing version cannot be amended, since they involve areas for which no notice of proposed change was given

prompt: Members of the Trenton Society disagree over a bylaw provision. One faction wants the assembly to 'reinterpret' a clause whose wording everyone concedes is perfectly clear, simply because they dislike the result. Advise whether the society may reinterpret a clear bylaw.
gold: Each society decides the meaning of its own bylaws, but an ambiguity must exist before there is any occasion for interpretation. Where the meaning is clear, the society cannot change that meaning, even by a unanimous vote, except by actually amending the bylaws. Since this clause is admittedly clear, the members cannot 'reinterpret' it; if they want a different result, they must amend the bylaw.

ref.quote: An ambiguity must exist before there is any occasion for interpretation

prompt: The bylaws of the Glenwood Society list its officers as a president, vice-president, secretary, treasurer, and directors. A member moves to elect a sergeant-at-arms, arguing the bylaws do not forbid it. Advise on whether the society may elect an officer not named in its bylaws.
gold: When bylaws specifically authorize certain things, other things of the same class are thereby prohibited. Because the bylaws enumerate the society's officers, the election of other officers not named, such as a sergeant-at-arms, is prohibited. The society cannot simply add such an officer by motion; it would first have to amend its bylaws to provide for that office.

ref.quote: the election of other officers not named, such as a sergeant-at-arms, is prohibited

prompt: The Ridgeway Club has just adopted a bylaw amendment raising the dues, and no one said anything about when it takes effect. The treasurer asks when the higher dues become operative, and whether the club could have delayed the effect. Advise.
gold: A bylaw amendment goes into effect immediately upon its adoption unless the motion to adopt specifies a different effective time, or the assembly has set one by a previously adopted motion. Since nothing was specified, the higher dues take effect at once. The club could have delayed the effect, but only by adopting a proviso or motion to that end while the amendment was pending or before voting on it.

ref.quote: An amendment to the bylaws goes into effect immediately upon its adoption unless the motion to adopt specifies another time for its becoming effective

prompt: At the Sutton Society's meeting, a bylaw amendment is adopted. A short time later, a member who voted for it has second thoughts and moves to reconsider the vote. Advise the chair on whether that motion is in order.
gold: The motion to amend the bylaws is subject to the ordinary rules for main motions, with certain exceptions, one of which is that an affirmative vote to amend the bylaws cannot be reconsidered. Because the amendment was adopted, the vote is not subject to reconsideration. The chair should rule the motion to reconsider out of order; if members want to undo the change, they must proceed by a new amendment with the required notice and vote.

ref.quote: An affirmative vote on the motion to amend the bylaws cannot be reconsidered

prompt: The Waverly Association maintains a separate constitution in addition to its bylaws and is drafting the amendment provisions for each. A committee member proposes making both amendable by the same two-thirds vote with notice. Advise on how the amendment requirements for the two documents should compare.
gold: Where a society keeps a constitution separate from its bylaws, the requirement for amending the constitution should be made more difficult than that for amending the bylaws; otherwise there is no point in having two separate documents. In either case, though, the vote required should be at least two-thirds. The committee should therefore set a higher hurdle for the constitution, for instance a larger vote or added notice, rather than making the two identical.

ref.quote: the requirement for amendment of the constitution should be made more difficult than that for amendment of the bylaws
